name: 1-2-4-All
desc: Engage everyone simultaneously in generating questions, ideas, and suggestions. You can immediately include everyone regardless of group size. Better ideas emerge faster, participants own them, and follow-through happens without the need for buy-in strategies.
when: Use instead of open discussion, brainstorming, or 'any questions?' after a presentation. Ideal whenever you want to replace one-way content delivery or discussions dominated by a few voices with genuine input from everyone.

phases.label: Alone
phases.instruction: Silently self-reflect on the question. Write down your ideas individually. No talking yet.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Take a quiet moment to yourself. What comes to mind? Jot down your thoughts — there are no wrong answers and no one will judge your notes.

phases.label: Pairs
phases.instruction: Share and develop ideas with one partner. Find common ground or interesting differences.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Find a partner and share what you wrote. Listen as much as you talk. What's new? What builds on your own thinking?

phases.label: Groups of 4
phases.instruction: Two pairs join together. Share the most interesting ideas and build on them.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Bring your pairs together. What did each pair discover? Look for patterns, surprises, and anything you disagree on.

phases.label: All
phases.instruction: Each group shares one key insight or idea with the whole room. Notice patterns and themes.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Each group offers one key idea to the whole room. Keep going until everything important is out — no repeats needed.

tips: Ask a single, crisp, open question. Vague questions produce vague answers — sharpen it before you start.
Protect the individual silence round. It is the most frequently skipped and the most important. 1 minute feels long. Let it.
In the All round, ask for one idea that has NOT yet been shared. This prevents repetition and forces groups to listen.
Time the rounds strictly — use a visible timer or a bell. Transitions are part of the structure.
Resist the urge to debrief after every round. Let the structure do the work.
For groups of 5 or 7, create a group of 3 rather than breaking pairs — trios work fine.
Do a second round if the first felt shallow. The second cycle benefits from what surfaced in the first.
Trap: letting the All round become a monologue from each group. Keep it to one idea per group, then ask 'who has something different?'
structuralInvitation: What is your thinking on [challenge/question]? Please reflect alone first.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Engage every individual in searching for answers
Avoid groupthink — preserve independent judgment before social influence sets in
Tap the know-how and imagination distributed widely across the group, not just the vocal few
Generate better ideas and more of them, faster than open discussion
Build shared ownership — participants own the ideas, so follow-through is simplified
Surface what is already known but unsaid
Include everyone regardless of group size
variations: After groups of 4, move to groups of 8 to build toward consensus — Liz Rykert calls this 'Octopus!'
Use with post-its and visual mapping so ideas become tangible and patterns emerge across the room
Do a second rapid cycle when the first didn't go deep enough — two 10-minute cycles beat one 20-minute one
Run multiple cycles with escalating questions, each building on the previous harvest
Combine with Impromptu Networking: use 1-2-4-All at the start to generate questions, then let pairs explore them in open networking
examplesOfUse: Immediately after a presentation or speech — replace passive Q&A with rich, distributed feedback
At the start of any meeting to surface concerns, questions, and ideas before the agenda locks in
During strategic planning sessions to generate options from everyone, not just the senior team
In classrooms and training sessions to engage all participants simultaneously
For front-line staff to surface improvement ideas that rarely reach leadership
In large conferences with hundreds of participants — works just as well at scale
To help a group work through a complex problem by building from individual reflection to collective insight
As a debrief structure after any experience or field visit

name: Impromptu Networking
desc: Rapidly and simultaneously share challenges and expectations, and build new connections. In a series of 2-minute conversations, participants prepare for the session ahead while making unexpected connections with others in the room.
when: Use at the start of a meeting, workshop, or conference to get people out of their seats, talking to strangers, and thinking about the challenge ahead. Particularly powerful when participants don't know each other or come from different departments.

phases.label: Round 1
phases.instruction: Find a partner you don't know well. Share: what is your biggest challenge with today's topic?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Frame the question clearly: 'In each short conversation, share your honest thinking on [the challenge]. You've got two minutes each direction — go.'

phases.label: Round 2
phases.instruction: New partner. Share: what do you most hope to contribute to today's session?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Find someone you haven't spoken to yet. Share your thinking, listen to theirs. Then move on.

phases.label: Round 3
phases.instruction: New partner. Share: what do you most want to learn or take away from today?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Same again — new person, same question, fresh perspective. Notice how your thinking shifts with each conversation.

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: Return to the whole group. What themes came up? What connections surprised you?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What surprised you? What kept coming up? Did your thinking shift at all after talking to different people?

tips: Give participants enough time to find new partners each round — 2 minutes feels rushed but works.
Use at least 3 rounds (6 minutes total). Fewer rounds don't generate enough connection.
Ask people to find someone they don't know or rarely speak to — make it explicit.
The question should invite genuine reflection, not just information exchange. 'What's your biggest challenge this year?' beats 'What's your role?'
Trap: participants gravitating to people they already know. Name this upfront and ask people to actively seek strangers.
In large groups (50+), no need to debrief — let the energy from the networking carry straight into the next activity.
Follow up with 1-2-4-All to harvest what emerged and build on it.
structuralInvitation: What is the most important challenge you hope we will address today? What do you most hope to contribute?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the network weaving work of June Holley.
whyPurposes: Initiate creative and innovative projects by quickly developing connections across silos
Explore issues and challenges before diving into formal work
Generate more good ideas faster by activating distributed thinking early
Create a sense of shared purpose and common ground at the start of a session
Help introverts engage — the 1-on-1 format with clear time limits removes social anxiety
Surface the unexpected connections and common ground that exist across a group
variations: Use a single, focused question for all conversations to build convergence on a theme
Use two questions in sequence — one about the challenge, one about what the person hopes to contribute
Make it completely open (no question) to surface whatever is top of mind for each person
Follow with 1-2-4-All to harvest themes from the conversations
In virtual settings, use breakout rooms with 2 people per room, randomised each round
examplesOfUse: Opening a conference or summit to activate the collective intelligence of the room before any presentations
Starting a cross-departmental working group where participants don't know each other
Kicking off a strategy day to surface what's really on people's minds
At the start of a training programme to help participants connect before content begins
Between a presentation and structured work time — as a transition that activates rather than passifies

name: Nine Whys
desc: Make the purpose of your work together clear by progressively revealing deeper motivations. By asking 'why?' repeatedly in pairs, participants surface the real reasons behind what they do — going from surface activity to fundamental purpose.
when: Use at the start of a project, initiative, or collaboration when purpose is unclear or assumed. Especially useful when a group is stuck on tactics rather than aligned on why the work matters.

phases.label: Pair up
phases.instruction: Find a partner. One person will ask 'why?' repeatedly, the other answers honestly.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Sit with a partner. One of you starts by saying what you do. The other just listens — and gets ready to ask one simple question.

phases.label: The nine whys
phases.instruction: Partner A: describe what you do at work. Partner B: ask 'why is that important to you?' after each answer. Repeat up to 9 times. Listen for what emerges.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Here is your only question for the next few minutes: 'And why is that important to you?' Ask it again after every answer. Don't rush — let the silence work.

phases.label: Switch
phases.instruction: Swap roles. Partner B shares, Partner A asks why repeatedly.
phases.friendlyInstruction: By now you have gone several layers deep. What is the real reason this matters? What showed up that you did not expect?

phases.label: Share themes
phases.instruction: In the whole group: what core purposes emerged? What surprised you about your own answers?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What was it like to be asked 'why' so many times? What surprised you about your own answers? Share the most meaningful thing you discovered.

tips: Demonstrate the structure with a volunteer before asking pairs to try it — people underestimate how powerful simple repetition is.
Coach pairs to ask the question exactly: 'And why is that important to you?' Not 'why?' alone — the full formulation matters.
Encourage silence after each answer. The good answers come after a pause.
Trap: pairs accepting the first or second 'why' and stopping. Push gently: 'And why is THAT important?'
Trap: answers becoming abstract or philosophical too quickly. Redirect back to the specific work.
The person answering often surprises themselves. Make space for that — don't rush to the next question.
Debrief with: 'What surprised you in your own answers? What emerged that you didn't expect?'
structuralInvitation: What do you do? (then keep asking) And why is that important to you?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Toyota "Five Whys" method.
whyPurposes: Help participants clarify and articulate the deeper purpose of their work
Reveal the 'why' behind strategies, projects, and everyday activities
Build shared understanding of what motivates different team members
Surface hidden assumptions about purpose that are driving — or blocking — progress
Connect individual motivation to collective purpose
Avoid wasted effort on work that doesn't connect to what truly matters
variations: Run multiple cycles, each starting from a different person's initial answer, to surface diverse starting points
Use written responses before the conversation to help quieter participants find their footing
Stop after 5 whys if the group reaches a genuinely deep answer — don't force all 9
Follow with 1-2-4-All: 'What themes in our deeper purposes suggest a shared foundation?'
Have each pair share their final 'why' — the deepest one — in the group debrief
examplesOfUse: Before launching a change initiative to ensure the team is aligned on why it truly matters
When a team feels disconnected from its work or is going through the motions
At the start of a strategy session to ground tactics in genuine purpose
In coaching conversations to help individuals reconnect with what drives them
When onboarding new members to a team — understanding individual motivations builds trust faster

name: Wicked Questions
desc: Articulate the paradoxical challenges that a group must confront to succeed. By naming the tensions explicitly — 'How is it that we pursue X and also the opposite?' — the group can stop pretending those tensions don't exist and start working with them honestly.
when: Use when a group is stuck between competing demands, when strategy feels contradictory, or when people are privately aware of a tension no one is naming. Especially useful before or after strategic planning.

phases.label: Generate
phases.instruction: Individually: what are the pairs of opposites that describe your challenge? Start with 'How is it that we...' and 'and at the same time...'
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about the real tensions in your work — things that feel contradictory but are both completely true. Start writing them as: 'How is it that we [do X] and at the same time [do the opposite]?'

phases.label: Share in small groups
phases.instruction: Share your wicked questions in groups of 4-6. Identify the sharpest, most provocative ones.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Now refine them. Do they feel uncomfortable? Do they capture a REAL tension? The best Wicked Questions make you wince a little — that is a good sign.

phases.label: Gallery share
phases.instruction: Each group shares their sharpest wicked question. Which ones get the biggest reaction? Those are the important ones.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Which question feels most alive — most important AND most uncomfortable? That is usually the one worth staying with as a group.

tips: A good Wicked Question makes people wince slightly. If everyone nods easily, it's not wicked enough.
The format is essential: 'How is it that we [pursue X] AND [pursue the exact opposite]?' Don't let it become just a question.
Both sides of the paradox must be real and valid — not a false dilemma.
Trap: groups trying to 'solve' the Wicked Questions. The point is to name and hold the tension, not resolve it.
Trap: questions that are actually just problems ('How is it that we have no budget?') rather than genuine paradoxes.
Use in strings with TRIZ, Min Specs, or 15% Solutions to move from naming tensions to acting within them.
structuralInvitation: How is it that we [pursue one strategy/value] and at the same time [pursue the exact opposite]?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Engage everyone in sharper strategic thinking by making real dilemmas visible
Surface the contradictions and double-binds that are quietly blocking progress
Create a shared vocabulary for tensions that everyone feels but no one names
Prevent the waste and frustration of strategies that ignore real paradoxes
Open the space for creative solutions that honour both sides of a tension
Build psychological safety — naming contradictions signals honesty and trust
variations: Have the whole group vote on which Wicked Question is most important to work with next
Use as an input to Min Specs: which tensions must be held in your minimum specifications?
Follow with TRIZ to identify what you are currently doing that makes the bad side of the paradox worse
Combine with 1-2-4-All to generate many Wicked Questions before selecting the most powerful
Post all questions visibly and let participants dot-vote for the most resonant
examplesOfUse: Before a strategic planning session to surface the real tensions the strategy must navigate
When a team is experiencing conflict that seems intractable — the conflict often masks a Wicked Question
In leadership development to help leaders see the paradoxes inherent in their role
During organisational change when people are being asked to maintain operations while transforming them
In communities where opposing values — efficiency and care, innovation and stability — are in tension

name: Appreciative Interviews
desc: Discover and build on the best of what already exists in your organisation or community. Through paired storytelling focused on peak experiences, participants surface the concrete conditions that enabled their best work — then ask what it would take to create more of those conditions.
when: Use when you want to understand what is working before addressing what isn't. Ideal for the start of change initiatives, culture work, or any situation where the deficit view has dominated and drained energy.

phases.label: Prepare questions
phases.instruction: Frame 2-3 questions about peak experiences: when were you most proud of our work? What made it possible? What was your contribution?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think of a moment when this work felt genuinely meaningful — when something clicked. Write 2–3 questions that would help someone else tell that kind of story.

phases.label: Pair interviews
phases.instruction: Interview each other. Listen for the specific conditions and actions that enabled the peak experience. Take notes.
phases.friendlyInstruction: One person asks, the other tells their story. Real listening here — not planning your next question, but actually following where the story goes. Switch after a few minutes.

phases.label: Share stories
phases.instruction: Each person shares their partner's story (not their own). What themes emerge? What conditions keep appearing?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Tell the group what your PARTNER shared, not your own story. What conditions keep coming up across all the stories? What made the best moments possible?

phases.label: Extract lessons
phases.instruction: What conditions and practices can we create more of? What made our best work possible?
phases.friendlyInstruction: If these conditions — the ones that made your best moments happen — could become more common, what would actually need to change? What is one thing you could start doing tomorrow?

tips: The interview question must ask for a specific story, not an opinion. 'Tell me about a time...' not 'What do you think about...'
Coaches listeners to ask follow-up questions that draw out more detail: 'What made that possible? Who else was involved?'
When sharing partner stories in the group, listeners often share more enthusiasm than the original storyteller — use this.
Trap: interviews drifting into problem discussion. Gently redirect: 'That's important — and what about the times it worked?'
Trap: stories that are too vague ('things were just better'). Push for concrete, specific moments.
The debrief question matters: 'What conditions made this success possible?' is more useful than 'What went well?'
structuralInvitation: Tell me about a time when you were at your best — when you were most proud of your contribution to [shared purpose]. What made it possible?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Appreciative Inquiry (David Cooperrider).
whyPurposes: Discover and build on concrete examples of what is working, rather than focusing on problems
Identify the specific conditions that enabled the group's best performance
Generate positive energy and motivation for change by reconnecting with genuine success
Help people see that solutions to current problems may already exist somewhere in the system
Surface local wisdom and knowledge that is rarely visible to leadership
Create a shared foundation of pride and possibility before tackling difficult challenges
variations: Use the full Appreciative Inquiry 4-D cycle: Discover, Dream, Design, Destiny
Have participants interview across silos — different departments, roles, or levels — for richer cross-boundary learning
Focus the interview question on a specific challenge: 'Tell me about a time this team handled conflict really well'
Follow with 1-2-4-All: 'What conditions appear repeatedly in our stories? How do we create more of them?'
In large groups, have pairs share partner stories in groups of 8 before the full harvest
examplesOfUse: At the start of a culture change initiative to ground the work in real examples of the desired culture already existing
When a team is demoralised — reconnecting with past success rebuilds agency and hope
In communities facing a difficult challenge — finding local examples of success builds confidence
As part of onboarding to help new members understand what the organisation is at its best
In service improvement work to identify what patient or user experiences to build on, not just fix

name: TRIZ
desc: Stop counterproductive activities and behaviours by imagining the worst possible outcome and working backwards. By generating a complete list of everything you could do to guarantee failure, groups surface what they are already doing that undermines their own success — and commit to stopping it.
when: Use when a group is stuck, when change initiatives keep failing, or when there is a sense that the organisation is getting in its own way. The most honest and often most hilarious way to surface self-defeating behaviour.

phases.label: Identify the worst outcome
phases.instruction: Introduce the challenge. Invite groups to identify the single most unwanted result — the worst possible outcome.
phases.friendlyInstruction: What would be the absolute worst result here? Name it clearly — that's what we're working backwards from.

phases.label: List what guarantees failure
phases.instruction: Each group uses 1-2-4-All to generate a complete list of everything they could do to guarantee achieving the worst outcome. No filter — the more specific the better.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All: what would you have to do to guarantee that terrible outcome? Be creative and specific. The more outrageous the list, the more honest and useful this becomes.

phases.label: Spot what you're already doing
phases.instruction: Each group uses 1-2-4-All to identify which items from their failure list they are already doing, even if unintentionally. Be honest.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Here's the uncomfortable part. Using 1-2-4-All: which of those failure-guaranteeing things are you actually already doing? Not intentionally — but honestly?

phases.label: Commit to stopping
phases.instruction: Each group uses 1-2-4-All to determine first steps that will help them stop each identified behaviour. What specifically will you stop doing?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All: for each item you identified, what's the first step to stop doing it? Not "we should try to maybe..." — actual commitments. What specifically changes, starting when?

phases.label: Share and debrief
phases.instruction: Groups share their stopping commitments. What surprised us? What was hardest to admit? What is the single most important thing to stop?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What surprised you most? What was hardest to admit out loud? What's the one thing you're most committed to stopping?

tips: Start with a VERY unwanted result — the more extreme, the more honest and energetic the lists become.
Step 3 (what are we already doing?) is the breakthrough. Make space for uncomfortable honesty without shaming.
Insist on specifics. Vague entries like 'poor communication' should become 'never share the agenda in advance'.
Trap: groups trying to add new initiatives instead of committing to stopping things. Hold the stopping frame firmly.
Trap: laughter becoming a way to avoid genuine commitment. Humour is welcome, but real decisions must emerge.
Confirm stopping commitments in the form of 'I will stop...' and 'We will stop...' — first person makes it real.
Check in with groups that look confused — the logic (inverse thinking) is unfamiliar and may need a second explanation.
structuralInvitation: Make a complete list of everything you could do to make sure we achieve the worst possible outcome for [challenge].
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by one element of the Russian engineering approach TRIZ (teoriya resheniya izobretatelskikh zadatch).
whyPurposes: Make it possible to stop counterproductive activities before adding new ones
Surface what everyone knows but no one says — the actions that guarantee failure
Create clarity about what must stop before progress is possible
Break through defensiveness by approaching criticism indirectly and with humour
Identify the specific behaviours and programmes that are actively working against stated goals
Free up energy and resources currently consumed by activities that produce the opposite of what is wanted
variations: Focus on a very specific unwanted result for sharper output ('guarantee our team never trusts each other')
Do a silent written round before group sharing to surface ideas people might not say out loud
Use 1-2-4-All within each TRIZ step for groups of 8 or more
Make the list public and permanent — post it visibly so the group can hold itself accountable
String with Appreciative Interviews: discover what has worked (AI), then use TRIZ to stop what undermines it
examplesOfUse: Before a strategy retreat to clear out the activities and behaviours that have been blocking past strategies
In culture change programmes when the desired culture keeps being undermined by existing practices
With leadership teams who are aware that their own behaviours are part of the problem
In quality improvement to identify what the system is doing to produce the defects it is trying to eliminate
As a retrospective with a team that keeps repeating the same problems despite good intentions

name: 15% Solutions
desc: Discover and focus on what each person has the freedom to do right now — without waiting for permission, resources, or the right conditions. Small actions taken today compound into significant change, and identifying them restores agency when groups feel stuck.
when: Use when people feel that change requires someone else's action, more resources, or better conditions. The antidote to waiting for permission. Best used after a group has named a challenge clearly and needs to shift from diagnosis to action.

phases.label: Identify your 15%
phases.instruction: Individually: what is the 15% of the challenge where you have freedom to act? What actions are in your direct control, right now, without needing anyone's permission?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Forget what needs approval, budget, or help from others for now. What could YOU personally do — starting this week — that would make a small but real difference? Write it down.

phases.label: Share in pairs
phases.instruction: Share your 15% with a partner. Help each other see possibilities you might be missing. Your partner asks: what else is within your reach?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Share your idea with your small group. Ask for honest feedback: is this genuinely doable? What might get in the way? How could you make it even more specific?

phases.label: Commit
phases.instruction: Share commitments with a small group. State: I will [action] by [date]. Make it small enough to actually do.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Name your commitment out loud: not 'I'll try to...' but 'I will [specific thing] by [specific date].' Someone write it down — that accountability helps.

tips: The question is precise: 'Where do you have freedom to act WITHOUT needing anyone's permission or additional resources?' Not 'what would help?'
Coach participants to be honest about what is actually within their control — solutions that need approval are NOT 15% Solutions.
Small is the point. 'Host one 30-minute conversation with my team' is better than 'transform our culture'.
Trap: groups inflating their solutions to 100% (grand plans requiring leadership buy-in). Challenge these gently.
Trap: participants dismissing small actions as too insignificant. The power is in the compounding, not the individual step.
Follow with Troika Consulting so others can help identify 15% Solutions that the person couldn't see themselves.
structuralInvitation: What is your 15%? Where do you have freedom to act right now, without needing anyone's permission or additional resources?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Restore agency — show that everyone has more freedom to act than they realise
Identify immediate actions that don't require approval, budget, or coordination
Avoid the paralysis of waiting for ideal conditions that never arrive
Build momentum through small wins that compound over time
Help individuals and groups see the difference between what they control and what they don't
Counter the learned helplessness that accumulates in large organisations
variations: Ask participants to physically exchange their 15% Solutions with a partner for feedback: 'Is this truly within your control?'
Follow with Troika Consulting so peers can help expand and strengthen each other's solutions
Use as the final step in a string (TRIZ + 1-2-4-All + 15% Solutions) — stop what doesn't work, then name what you can start
In teams: 'What is our collective 15%?' — what can the team do together without escalating?
Set a 30-day follow-up: each person reports back on what they actually did
examplesOfUse: At the end of any session where a group has identified challenges — shift from diagnosis to action
With demoralised teams who feel powerless — naming what they CAN do rebuilds energy
In public sector contexts where budget and authority constraints are real but often overstated
After a Nine Whys session to move from purpose into immediate action
In coaching or mentoring conversations to identify the next concrete step

name: Troika Consulting
desc: Develop better solutions and have more good ideas by getting help from peers who are not trapped in the same constraints as you. Three people take turns: one brings a real challenge, two consult without the client present, then the client hears what emerged.
when: Use when individuals have a genuine challenge they're wrestling with and need fresh thinking. The absence of the client while consultants speak is what generates insight — it removes social pressure and creates honest analysis.

phases.label: Form trios
phases.instruction: Get into groups of exactly 3. Decide who goes first as the 'client'.
phases.friendlyInstruction: You have got 1–2 minutes. Tell your troika: what is the challenge you are wrestling with right now? Be specific — the more concrete the better. They will just listen for now.

phases.label: Client presents
phases.instruction: The client shares their challenge or question in 2-3 minutes. Consultants listen without interrupting.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Client — turn away or close your eyes. Consultants: what do you think is really going on here? What ideas or questions come to mind? Talk openly — the client is listening but not responding.

phases.label: Consultants confer
phases.instruction: Client turns their back or mutes themselves. The two consultants discuss and generate suggestions, insights, or questions. Client listens silently.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Client — turn back around. What landed for you? What surprised you? What will you take away, even if it was just a word or a reframe?

phases.label: Client reflects
phases.instruction: Client turns back and shares what was most useful. Then rotate — next person becomes the client.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Switch! Each person gets a turn in the client chair. Same energy — be honest, be curious, be generous with each other.

tips: The client must bring a REAL, current challenge — not a hypothetical or one already solved.
The most important moment: the client turns away (or leaves the room). Consultants speak more freely and honestly when not performing for the client.
Consultants should resist jumping to advice. Begin with: 'What do we think is really going on here?'
When the client returns, they reflect — they don't argue or explain. They just say what landed.
Trap: consultants giving unsolicited advice before genuinely understanding the challenge.
Trap: clients choosing a challenge that is 'safe' rather than the one that is really keeping them up at night.
Time discipline is essential — without it, the first client gets 20 minutes and the last gets 2.
structuralInvitation: What is your most important challenge for which you could use the help and insight of your peers?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Help each other solve difficult problems
Give and receive help without the dependency and passivity that expert advice often creates
Build coaching skills among non-specialists
Create a culture of peer support and mutual accountability
Surface insights that the person closest to the problem cannot see
Develop trust and candour in a team or community of practice
variations: Use written 'postcards' so consultants can share unfiltered thoughts without social inhibition
Increase group size to 4 for complex challenges (Troika Consulting becomes Wise Crowds)
In virtual settings, the client leaves the breakout room while consultants work — then rejoins
Follow with 15% Solutions: what will the client do with what they heard?
String three Troika rounds back-to-back so everyone gets a turn as client in one session
examplesOfUse: In leadership development programmes to develop peer coaching habits
In communities of practice where practitioners help each other with real cases
At the end of a project or initiative to help leaders navigate what comes next
In team retrospectives when individuals have specific stuck points they haven't named to the group
With front-line staff who solve each other's problems informally every day — this makes it deliberate

name: What, So What, Now What? W³
desc: Together, look back on progress and highlights to extract lessons and guide next steps. Moving through three stages — observable facts, patterns and meaning, and actions — prevents the most common failure of retrospectives: jumping to action before understanding what actually happened.
when: Use after any significant shared experience — a project phase, a field visit, a difficult meeting, a training programme, or a major event. Particularly valuable when a group has gone through something difficult and needs to process it collectively before deciding what to do next.

phases.label: What?
phases.instruction: Individuals work 1 min alone, then 2-7 min in small groups. Ask: "What happened? What did you observe? What facts or data stood out?" Collect salient facts with the whole group (2-3 min). Strictly observable only — no interpretations yet. Note that emotions can be observed as data (e.g. "people were smiling") rather than inferred (e.g. "people were happy").
phases.friendlyInstruction: Start alone for 1 minute: what actually happened? What did you see, hear, or notice? Facts and observations only — things you could have filmed. Then share in small groups, then collect key observations as a whole group. If someone starts interpreting, gently redirect: "That's a great So What — let's hold it for the next round."

phases.label: So What?
phases.instruction: Individuals work 1 min alone, then 2-7 min in small groups. Ask: "Why is that important? What patterns or conclusions are emerging? What hypotheses can you make?" This is the most difficult stage — it's where meaning is made. Collect key themes with the whole group (2-3 min).
phases.friendlyInstruction: Now the harder question: why does it matter? Work alone first (1 min), then in small groups. What patterns do you see in those observations? What does it mean for us? This is the most important stage — don't rush it. Then share the most significant patterns as a whole group.

phases.label: Now What?
phases.instruction: Individuals work 1 min alone, then 2-7 min in small groups. Ask: "What actions make sense now? What will you do differently?" Collect commitments with the whole group.
phases.friendlyInstruction: So what will you actually do? Work alone first (1 min): given what we've observed and what it means, what actions make sense? Then share in small groups and surface the most important commitments to the whole group.

phases.label: Commitments
phases.instruction: Collect specific commitments. Each person or group states what they will do differently. Make commitments public and specific — who does what by when.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Name your commitment out loud — that makes it real. Who does what by when? Vague intentions don't count here. One specific thing from each person or group.

tips: Enforce the order strictly. Groups want to jump to Now What. Don't allow it until So What is complete.
The What round must be purely observational — facts and data only. Challenge interpretations: 'Is that something you observed, or a conclusion you drew?'
The So What round is the hardest and most important. Give it more time than feels comfortable.
Trap: the What round becoming a story retelling from one person's perspective. Ask for DIFFERENT observations, not more of the same.
Trap: Now What producing vague intentions rather than specific commitments. Ask 'who does what by when?'
Validate emotions as data during What — 'I noticed I felt uncomfortable' is an observation.
structuralInvitation: WHAT? What happened? What did you notice, what facts or observations stood out for you?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Ladder of Inference (Chris Argyris, 1982; Peter Senge, 1990).
whyPurposes: Facilitate group learning from experience without jumping prematurely to conclusions
Build shared understanding of what happened before the group tries to interpret it
Prevent the common trap of letting the loudest voice define what the experience meant
Help groups distinguish between observation and interpretation — a skill that transfers beyond this exercise
Generate actions that are grounded in genuine insight rather than reactive impulse
Make reflection a regular, structured part of the group's practice
variations: In virtual settings, use digital sticky notes for each round — participants place them in columns: What / So What / Now What
Run it as a written exercise first, then share in small groups before harvesting with the whole group
Use after field visits or ethnographic observation to process raw observations together
Run multiple short cycles (5 minutes each) rather than one long debrief for time-pressured groups
Combine with WINFY: use W³ to process what happened, then use WINFY to name what each group needs from others to act
examplesOfUse: After a project milestone or sprint review to extract learning before moving on
Following a difficult conversation, conflict, or organisational incident
At the end of a training programme to consolidate learning and generate commitments
After a site visit or field observation to turn raw experience into insight
As a regular team retrospective format — replacing unstructured 'what went well/what could improve'

name: Discovery & Action Dialogue
desc: Discover, spread, and implement local solutions to chronic problems. Six specific questions help groups uncover what individuals already know, what gets in the way, and what small actions are possible right now — without importing solutions from outside.
when: Use when the same problem persists despite multiple external interventions, when front-line workers have insights that aren't reaching decision-makers, or when you want to spread a local solution more widely.

phases.label: Introduce the six questions
phases.instruction:
The facilitator introduces the six DAD questions that will guide the dialogue. These are fixed — do not skip or modify them:
1. How do you know when [problem] is present?
2. How do you contribute to [problem] occurring, even if unintentionally?
3. What do you do — or could you do — to address [problem]?
4. What gets in the way of doing this all the time?
5. Who else should be involved in creating solutions?
6. What needs to happen to get started?
phases.friendlyInstruction: There are six questions that guide this conversation — and they're fixed for a reason. Each one unlocks a different kind of honest conversation. The facilitator reads them out; take a moment to let them land before we dive in.

phases.label: Small group dialogue
phases.instruction:
In groups of 6-8, work through all six questions. A facilitator guides each group through the sequence:
1. How do you know when [the problem] is present? (observable signs)
2. How do you contribute to this problem, even if unintentionally?
3. What do you do, or could you do, to address the problem?
4. What gets in the way of doing this all the time?
5. Who else should be involved?
6. What needs to happen to get started? What is your first step?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Work through all six questions in your small group. Take your time — especially on question 2 (your own contribution) and question 3 (what actually helps). Question 3 is where the local solutions live. Question 4 is where the real obstacles are. Don't rush past either.

phases.label: Harvest actions
phases.instruction: Each group shares: one thing we will try (a specific action), and one thing we need (support or resource). Make commitments specific and realistic. Record them visibly.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Each group shares two things: one thing you will actually try — specific enough to know if you've done it — and one thing you need to make it possible. No vague intentions. Write them down publicly.

tips: The six questions are the structure — do not skip, reorder, or modify them. Each serves a specific purpose.
Question 2 (your own contribution to the problem) is the hardest. It requires genuine psychological safety. Don't rush past it.
Question 3 (what you do that helps) is where the local solutions live. Give it space — the gold is here.
Facilitators must be genuinely curious, not seeking to confirm what they already think.
Trap: groups answering what 'should' be done rather than what they actually do. Keep redirecting to the specific and concrete.
Trap: focusing only on what others should do rather than what the person themselves can do.
Follow up. Commitments without accountability rarely stick.
structuralInvitation: How do you know when [the problem] is present? What do you do that helps, even in small ways? What gets in the way?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Positive Deviance methods.
whyPurposes: Discover local solutions to seemingly intractable problems that outsiders cannot see
Spread effective practices across groups, teams, and communities without top-down mandates
Engage front-line workers as experts in the problems they face daily
Build ownership — solutions that are discovered locally are implemented locally
Bridge the gap between what people know and what they do
Surface the specific obstacles that prevent people from doing what they know works
variations: Run multiple DAD sessions with different groups, then use 1-2-4-All across groups to identify common patterns
Use an outside facilitator who is genuinely curious rather than already knowing the answer
In large groups, run simultaneous DAD sessions then harvest across groups
Combine with Positive Deviance inquiry: use DAD to find the 'positive deviants' already solving the problem
Follow up at 30 and 60 days to support the actions that were identified
examplesOfUse: In healthcare to discover why some wards have much lower infection rates than others
In schools to understand why some students succeed where others don't — and what teachers are doing differently
In communities to surface what some families are doing to keep children healthy that others could adopt
In organisations to understand how some teams maintain morale through change while others don't
In public services to surface the informal workarounds that front-line staff use to serve clients well

name: Shift & Share
desc: Spread good ideas and make informal connections between innovators. In rapid cycles, people share work in progress with rotating groups — creating a marketplace of ideas where the audience comes to the presenter rather than the other way around.
when: Use when there is innovative work happening across a group or organisation that people don't know about. Replaces the passive conference presentation with active, peer-to-peer learning. Best when you have 5-10 people with distinct innovations to share.

phases.label: Set up stations
phases.instruction: Innovators set up their stations around the room with materials, examples, or prototypes of their work.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Set up your station with your materials. You will be presenting your work in short bursts to rotating groups — think of it as a mini marketplace of ideas.

phases.label: Round 1
phases.instruction: Groups of 5-8 visit their first station. Innovator shares their work and answers questions.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Visitors arrive. Walk them through your work in 3–5 minutes, then open it up: what questions do they have? What connections do they see? This is a conversation, not a lecture.

phases.label: Round 2
phases.instruction: Groups shift to next station. Fresh presentation, fresh questions.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Rotate! As a visitor, your job is to learn, make connections, and bring ideas back to your own work. What can you adapt?

phases.label: Round 3
phases.instruction: Groups shift to final station.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Reconvene. What was the most interesting thing you saw? What idea from someone else's work could you actually use?

phases.label: Harvest
phases.instruction: Whole group: what are the most transferable insights? What surprised you? What will you try?

tips: Presenters need at least 3 minutes of preparation time — they are sharing work in progress, not a finished product.
Rotate audiences rather than presenters — it's much easier to move the crowd than to break down and reassemble presentations.
Encourage presenters to ask a specific question of each visiting group: 'What would you do differently?' or 'How could this apply to your context?'
Trap: presenters reverting to formal presentation mode. Remind them: this is a conversation, not a talk.
Trap: some stations getting all the traffic while others are ignored. Facilitate movement to less-visited stations.
Works best with 5-7 presenters and group sizes of 5-8 per round.
structuralInvitation: What innovative work do you have to share with others that they could adapt and learn from?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Spread innovations and good practices that exist locally but haven't been shared widely
Enable genuine peer-to-peer learning without central coordination or gatekeeping
Give innovators recognition and feedback from people doing similar work
Expose people to ideas they didn't know to look for
Build connections between people with complementary approaches
Replace passive conference-style presentations with active, engaged exchange
variations: Use visual displays, prototypes, or demos rather than just talking — make it a genuine marketplace
Allow audience members to move freely rather than in structured rotations — Open Space within Shift & Share
Invite presenters to ask the audience for help with a specific challenge rather than just showcasing success
Run multiple rounds so presenters get feedback and can refine between sessions
Follow with 1-2-4-All: 'What is the one idea from another station that you could adapt for your own work?'
examplesOfUse: At a staff conference to share innovative projects happening across departments
In a network of organisations to cross-pollinate practices between sites
In schools for teachers to share pedagogical innovations with each other
At a community of practice gathering where members are working on related but distinct problems
In an innovation programme where teams are at the prototype stage and need peer feedback

name: 25/10 Crowd Sourcing
desc: Rapidly generate and sift a group's most actionable and bold ideas. By having participants write ideas on cards, pass them randomly, and score each card they receive, the crowd surfaces its best ideas without any central control or voting manipulation.
when: Use when you need to generate bold ideas from a large group quickly and find the ones with the most energy. Replaces voting systems that can be gamed, and works with groups of 10 to 1,000+.

phases.label: Write bold ideas
phases.instruction: Everyone writes their boldest idea on a card. Ask: if you had 10x the resources, what would you do?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Write your boldest idea on a card — the one you would pitch if you were 10 times braver than usual. Make it specific enough that someone else could understand and rate it.

phases.label: Pass and score
phases.instruction: Pass cards around randomly. Score each card 1-5 as you receive it. Keep passing every 30 seconds until you've scored 5+ cards.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Swap cards with whoever is near you. Read the card, rate it 1–5 on the back, then swap again. Keep moving — this is fast.

phases.label: Count and reveal
phases.instruction: Add up the scores on your current card. Call out the top scores. The highest 10% are read aloud.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Keep swapping and scoring. You are building a crowd-sourced ranking without anyone knowing whose idea is whose.

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: What surprised you? Which ideas deserve further exploration? What do the top ideas have in common?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Call time. The ideas with the highest total scores bubble up. What are the top ideas? Does anything surprise you about what the crowd valued?

tips: The idea must be specific enough that someone could read it cold and understand what it means.
Use physical index cards in person — the tactile random swap is part of what makes it work.
Each card is scored 1-5 independently of all others. There is no relative ranking between cards during scoring.
Participants score the card they are HOLDING, not the ones they wrote — anonymity is preserved.
Trap: participants hesitating to score a 1 or 5. Encourage the full range — avoid clustering around 3.
Trap: participants writing the same idea on every card to game the system. Address this upfront.
In groups smaller than 10, do a second round of passing to ensure cards are well-shuffled.
structuralInvitation: If you were 10 times bolder, what big idea would you recommend we act on? Write your boldest idea on a card.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Open Space Technology.
whyPurposes: Generate creative solutions beyond what a small group or planning committee could produce
Tap the wisdom and boldness of everyone in the room simultaneously
Surface the ideas with the most energy and potential without political influence
Build shared ownership of the best ideas — the group generated them
Create a ranked list of actionable proposals in minutes rather than hours
Reveal surprising consensus on priorities that formal voting processes often obscure
variations: Use two questions in sequence — first 'what would you do if resources were no obstacle?' then 'what is your 10% more bold version of your idea?'
Project running scores in real time for large groups
In virtual settings, use shared digital cards with anonymous scoring
Follow with 1-2-4-All to deepen the top ideas before committing to action
Use the top-scoring ideas as inputs to a Design StoryBoard or Purpose-to-Practice session
examplesOfUse: At the end of a strategy session to identify the boldest ideas the whole group is ready to act on
In a large community meeting to surface priorities from hundreds of people in 20 minutes
In innovation challenges to select the most promising ideas to prototype
As a rapid decision-making tool when a group has too many options and needs to converge
In conferences to generate 'next steps' from the entire audience rather than just the platform

name: Wise Crowds
desc: Tap the collective and distributed intelligence of your whole group at once. Multiple clients present their real challenges simultaneously to rotating small groups of consultants, dramatically multiplying the depth and diversity of advice available to each person.
when: Use when multiple people have challenges that would benefit from fresh, collective thinking and there isn't time for individual coaching or a Troika Consulting session for everyone. Scales peer consulting across a large group efficiently.

phases.label: Form groups
phases.instruction: Form groups of 4-5. Each group will work through Troika Consulting so everyone gets a turn as client.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about the challenge you most need help with right now. Something real — not a hypothetical. Prepare to describe it in about 2 minutes.

phases.label: Rotating Troika rounds
phases.instruction: Each person takes a turn as client (3 min to present challenge), then turns away while the group consults (8 min), then receives feedback (4 min). Rotate until everyone has been client.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Client presents their challenge. Consultants: your job is to listen without preparing your response. Just listen.

phases.label: Synthesis
phases.instruction: Bring the whole group together. What themes appeared across challenges? What collective wisdom emerged? What actions will people take?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Client steps back from the circle. Consultants discuss: what is really going on? What questions would help? What ideas come to mind? Speak freely — the client is listening but silent.

tips: Clients must bring a REAL challenge — not a hypothetical or a solved problem.
Time the client's presentation strictly: 2 minutes maximum. Consultants spend the rest of the time working.
The debrief when the client returns: what resonated? What will you do? Not: do you agree with the consultants?
Trap: consultants spending most of the time asking clarifying questions rather than consulting.
Trap: clients giving long background. Encourage them to prepare: 'What is the real challenge, in one sentence?'
For deep challenges, a second consulting round after the initial debrief can go much further than the first.
structuralInvitation: What is your most important challenge for which you could use the collective wisdom of everyone in this room?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Provide everyone with access to the wisdom and experience of the whole group
Develop everyone's capacity to give and receive help
Make confidential challenges discussable in a safe and structured way
Build the peer coaching culture without requiring formal coaching training
Multiply the impact of group intelligence — parallel rather than sequential consulting
Help people see their challenges from multiple fresh perspectives simultaneously
variations: In large groups, have the client stand at a fixed station while consultant groups rotate — this is more efficient
Use written postcards as in Troika Consulting to ensure consultants share honest views
Allow clients to choose a panel of consultants rather than random assignment — useful when specific expertise matters
Follow with 15% Solutions — each client identifies their next step immediately after receiving input
Use a dedicated fishbowl to debrief what themes emerged across multiple client challenges
examplesOfUse: In leadership programmes to help participants apply learning to real current challenges
In communities of practice where members have similar but distinct problems
At team retreats to address accumulated individual challenges before collective planning
In peer supervision groups for professionals (teachers, nurses, social workers) with complex cases
As a regular team practice — rotating 'client of the week' who brings a challenge for collective consultation

name: Min Specs
desc: Specify only the absolute must-dos and must-not-dos for achieving your purpose — and by eliminating everything else, create the freedom to innovate within a meaningful minimum of constraints. Min Specs liberate more than they constrain.
when: Use when a group needs to agree on non-negotiable parameters before acting autonomously, when constraints are unclear or excessive, or when people are waiting for permission to act because the rules are unclear.

phases.label: Generate Max Specs
phases.instruction: In the context of a challenging activity, new initiative, or strategic bottleneck, invite participants to generate the complete list of all must-dos and must-not-dos. Work alone for 1 minute, then consolidate and expand in the small group for 5 minutes. Make the list as complete as possible.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think of everything you believe you must do and must never do to achieve your purpose. Start alone (1 min), then share and expand in your small group (5 min). Get everything on the table — don't filter yet. This is your Max Specs list.

phases.label: Test each spec
phases.instruction: Each small group tests every item on their Max Specs list against the purpose statement using this test: "If we broke or ignored this rule, could we still achieve our purpose?" If the answer is yes, the spec is dropped from the list.
phases.friendlyInstruction: For each rule on your list, ask: if we broke this rule, could we still achieve our purpose? If yes — cross it off. Be ruthless. You're looking for the rules that are truly non-negotiable, not just comfortable habits.

phases.label: Second round if needed
phases.instruction: Do a second round of testing if needed. Groups continue challenging each item. Often two to five Min Specs are sufficient — if your list is still longer, keep testing.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Still too many rules on the list? Keep going. The goal is 2-5 truly essential specs — the ones that, if broken, would genuinely prevent you from achieving your purpose. Everything else is optional.

phases.label: Compare and consolidate
phases.instruction: Compare Min Specs across small groups and consolidate to the shortest shared list. Challenge any spec that appears in only one group's list.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Share your minimal lists across groups. Where do you agree? Where does one group have a rule others don't? Challenge those — are they truly essential or just one team's preference?

phases.label: Commit
phases.instruction: Agree on the final minimal set. Announce: everything else is permitted — people are free to innovate within these constraints.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Here are your Min Specs — the true non-negotiables. Everything else is allowed. You now have maximum freedom within minimum constraint. Go wild responsibly.

tips: The test for each spec is merciless: 'If we broke this rule, could we still achieve our purpose?' If yes, it is NOT a Min Spec.
Groups typically start with 15-20 rules and end with 2-5. Trust the reduction process even when it feels uncomfortable.
Both 'must-do' and 'must-not-do' specs are valid and important.
Trap: confusing Min Specs with best practices or preferences. Only genuine non-negotiables survive the test.
Trap: groups keeping specs for comfort or habit rather than necessity. Hold the test firmly.
The final list should feel both minimal AND complete — if it feels like something important is missing, test whether it truly belongs.
structuralInvitation: What must we absolutely do and must we absolutely never do to achieve our purpose? Generate a complete list, then reduce it to the true minimum.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Define the boundaries of what is absolutely required without over-specifying how people work
Create maximum freedom within minimum necessary constraint
Surface hidden or unexamined rules that are blocking progress
Ensure that essential requirements are explicit, shared, and agreed
Stop the proliferation of guidelines and procedures that add complexity without adding value
Enable autonomous action while maintaining alignment on what truly matters
variations: Start with Max Specs (list everything) to make visible the full weight of existing rules before testing each
Use multiple small groups to generate separate lists, then compare and challenge each other's specs
Apply to a specific process or initiative — 'What are the Min Specs for how we run our weekly meetings?'
Combine with Purpose-to-Practice to move from purpose through principles to minimum specifications
Post the final Min Specs visibly and review them at the next project milestone
examplesOfUse: Before delegating to a team — what are the absolute non-negotiables for how they work?
In regulatory or compliance contexts to distinguish essential requirements from bureaucratic accretion
When designing a new initiative — what must it do and must it never do to succeed?
In communities developing shared norms — 'what are the minimum agreements that allow us to function together?'
When streamlining processes that have accumulated unnecessary steps over time

name: Improv Prototyping
desc: Improve solutions by using improv theatre techniques to act out challenges and prototype better responses. Repeated cycles of acting, observing, and improving allow groups to discover and embody better behaviours — not just discuss them.
when: Use when a group needs to change how it handles recurring situations, when talk-based approaches have failed to shift behaviour, or when the challenge involves interpersonal dynamics that are hard to describe but easy to show.

phases.label: Explain and set the stage
phases.instruction: Explain what will be done and describe the sequence of steps (2 min). Set the stage by describing the scenario that will be acted out and the various roles (3 min). Invite 2-4 players to come forward.
phases.friendlyInstruction: First, walk everyone through what's about to happen — no surprises. Then set the scene: what's the situation we're going to play out? Who are the characters? Invite a few brave volunteers to come forward as the first players.

phases.label: Enact the scene
phases.instruction: Players on stage enact the scene (3-5 min). The scene should depict the challenge as it actually happens — not an idealised version. Other participants observe in small groups, each group looking for specific things.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Players: just act it out as it really happens. Don't try to show the ideal version — show the real one, warts and all. Observers: watch closely. What's working, even slightly? What's definitely not working? Your group will discuss this in a moment.
phases.groups: Players on stage; observers in small groups

phases.label: Small group debrief
phases.instruction: Each small observer group uses 1-2-4-All to identify successful and unsuccessful "chunks" from the scene they just observed. What specific moments or behaviours worked? What didn't?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All in your small group: what were the specific moments that worked — even slightly? What clearly didn't? You're looking for concrete chunks of behaviour, not general impressions.

phases.label: Build improved prototype
phases.instruction: Each observer group pieces together the successful chunks into a new prototype. Volunteers from within the group act out the new prototype for their own small group only (not the whole room yet). Refine it.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Your group now builds an improved version by keeping what worked and replacing what didn't. Act it out among yourselves — not for the whole room yet. Try things. Laugh. Adjust. What's better now?

phases.label: Share best prototype
phases.instruction: Participants from the group with the most improved prototype volunteer to come on stage and enact their version for the whole group (3-5 min). Continue with as many rounds as needed to arrive at one or more prototypes good enough to put into practice.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Which group has a version they're proud of? Come on stage and show us. The rest of us watch: what's genuinely better? What else could change? Keep going until you have something worth actually trying in real life.

tips: The scenes must be based on real recurring challenges the group faces — invented scenarios generate less insight.
Encourage exaggeration in the first round — it makes patterns visible and creates psychological safety.
Observer groups must be specific about what they saw, not what they felt: 'The manager interrupted three times' not 'the manager was rude'.
Trap: groups talking about the scene instead of acting it. Redirect: 'Show us — start acting.'
Trap: participants being too polite to show the problem as it really is. Name this: 'The real version, please.'
Multiple short rounds produce more insight than one long scene — keep momentum through iteration.
structuralInvitation: What frustrating recurring challenge would benefit from being acted out so we can discover better ways to handle it together?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Antonas Mockus (former mayor of Bogota) and Improv artists.
whyPurposes: Engage three types of knowledge simultaneously: explicit (what we say), tacit (what we observe), and latent (what emerges)
Prototype solutions to complex social and behavioural challenges that resist verbal description
Make invisible patterns of interaction visible and subject to improvement
Build new behaviours through embodied practice rather than intellectual understanding
Generate the unexpected insights that only arise when people act rather than talk
Create psychological safety for experimenting with new approaches in a low-stakes environment
variations: Invite the group to shout 'Freeze!' when they see something worth analysing — pause the scene mid-action
Have groups develop competing prototypes of the same scene, then act them all out and compare
Use video recording to allow groups to review their prototypes between rounds
Swap roles between insiders and outsiders — a manager plays the front-line worker, and vice versa
Follow with Design StoryBoards to map the most effective prototype into a visual process
examplesOfUse: In healthcare to prototype how clinical teams handle difficult conversations with patients
In service design to embody the customer journey and reveal friction points that don't show up in data
In leadership development to practise difficult conversations rather than just discuss them
In community conflict resolution to find new ways of engaging across disagreement
In training programmes where the goal is behaviour change, not just knowledge transfer

name: Helping Heuristics
desc: Practise and develop four progressive methods for helping others: Expert, Doctor, Process Consultant, and Facilitator. By naming and practising the different positions, groups discover when each is appropriate and how to avoid the most common trap — defaulting to expert advice when something else is needed.
when: Use in leadership development, coaching training, or any context where people want to improve how they help each other. Particularly valuable when teams rely too heavily on one mode of helping (usually expert advice) and need to expand their repertoire.

phases.label: Introduce the four positions
phases.instruction:
Introduce and demonstrate the four helping positions:
1. Expert — you give information or do the task for them
2. Doctor — you diagnose, then prescribe a solution
3. Process Consultant — you help them solve their own problem, without telling them what to do
4. Facilitator — you help the group help itself
Each position is appropriate in different situations. The trap is defaulting to Expert or Doctor when a different position would serve better.
phases.friendlyInstruction:
There are four ways to help someone, and most of us default to the first two:
1. Expert — "here's the answer"
2. Doctor — "here's what's wrong, here's my prescription"
3. Process Consultant — "let me help you figure it out yourself"
4. Facilitator — "let me help the group figure it out together"
Watch a demonstration of each. Notice when each position is most useful — and when it's overused.

phases.label: Practise in trios
phases.instruction: In groups of 3: one person brings a real challenge (client), one practices helping (helper), one observes (observer). The helper must start with curiosity — asking questions to understand before offering anything. Try to move through the positions progressively, only advancing when the previous position is insufficient. Observer gives specific feedback after.
phases.friendlyInstruction: In trios: one person brings a real challenge, one tries to help, one watches. Helper: you must start with genuine curiosity. Ask questions. Resist the urge to jump to Expert or Doctor mode immediately. Move through the positions only as needed. Observer: give honest, specific feedback on what you saw.

phases.label: Rotate roles
phases.instruction: Rotate so each person gets a turn in each role. Switch client and helper at minimum.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Switch roles. Everyone should experience being the client, the helper, and the observer.

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: Debrief as a whole group: Which position did helpers default to? When was each position most useful? What was hardest about holding back advice? What will you do differently in your next helping conversation?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What position did helpers default to? What was hardest about staying curious before jumping to answers? When was the Expert position actually the right one? What changes in how you'll help people going forward?

tips: Start by naming the four positions clearly before any practice — the framework must be visible throughout.
The most common default is Expert, followed by Doctor. Most people never reach Process Consultant without deliberate practice.
Coach observers to be specific: 'You jumped to a solution at minute 2' is more useful than 'you were too directive'.
Trap: helpers giving advice while calling it process consulting. Watch for advice disguised as questions ('Have you tried...?').
Trap: the person with the challenge choosing a safe, low-stakes issue. Encourage real challenges.
Debrief with: 'What position do you default to? When is that useful, and when does it get in the way?'
structuralInvitation: How can you help someone without taking over? What does the person in front of you actually need from you right now?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Edgar Schein's work on helping relationships.
whyPurposes: Practise progressive methods for giving and receiving help
Understand when each of the four helping positions is appropriate
Avoid the most common helping trap: jumping to expert advice before understanding what is needed
Build a shared vocabulary for different types of helping in a team or organisation
Develop the capacity to ask better questions before offering solutions
Create a culture where help is genuinely useful rather than dependency-creating
variations: Use real challenges from participants rather than invented scenarios — the insight is deeper
Add a fifth position: Witness/Presence — simply being present with someone in difficulty without trying to fix anything
Have observers score each interaction on a matrix: which position was used, when, and how effective was it?
Rotate observer, client, and helper roles so everyone experiences all three perspectives
Follow with Troika Consulting to practise process consulting immediately
examplesOfUse: In manager development programmes where leaders need to shift from 'expert answer-giver' to 'capacity builder'
In clinical and social care settings where the helper/helped dynamic has significant power implications
In communities of practice to develop peer mentoring skills
In organisations implementing coaching cultures to give the concept practical form
In consulting training to help practitioners understand when to offer expertise and when to hold back

name: Conversation Café
desc: Engage everyone in making sense of profound challenges. Using a talking piece and a series of conversational rounds, participants reflect deeply on complex issues without pressure to reach conclusions or fix anything — the conversation itself is the output.
when: Use when a group needs to make sense of a complex or contentious issue together, when there is deep disagreement that needs to be understood rather than resolved, or when the group has been through a significant shared experience and needs to process it collectively.

phases.label: Check-in
phases.instruction: Each person shares one word or sentence in response to the central question. No discussion yet.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Welcome everyone. The question we are exploring together today is [question]. There are a few simple agreements: listen to understand, speak from your own experience, and allow silence.

phases.label: Round 1 — open
phases.instruction: Open dialogue. Anyone can speak. Focus on understanding each other, not winning arguments. Use a talking object if helpful.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Round 1: everyone speaks once on the question, briefly. No discussion yet — just your initial take. The talking piece moves around the circle.

phases.label: Round 2 — deeper
phases.instruction: Deepen the conversation. What assumptions are we making? What are we not saying? What would change our minds?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Round 2 and 3: now it opens up. Build on what others have said, change your mind, share what struck you. The talking piece still goes to whoever holds it.

phases.label: Harvest
phases.instruction: What have we learned? What questions remain open? What surprised us? Share with the whole group.
phases.friendlyInstruction: What themes and insights emerged? What surprised you? What question are you leaving with that you did not arrive with?

phases.label: Check-out
phases.instruction: One word or sentence each: what are you leaving with?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What will you take away from this conversation? Not conclusions — just what shifted or opened up for you.

tips: The agreements for the Café must be established and genuinely accepted before starting — they are the container.
The host's role is to invite, not direct. Ask open questions, then get out of the way.
Talking pieces work — even in groups that find them unfamiliar. Give people permission to feel awkward.
Trap: the conversation becoming a debate where people argue positions. Redirect to experience: 'What is this like for you?'
Trap: one or two people dominating even with the talking piece. The host may need to actively invite quieter voices.
Do not force a conclusion. The value is in the conversation itself, not what is decided at the end.
structuralInvitation: What do you think about [complex topic]? What meaning does it have for you?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Conversation Café method developed by Vicki Robin and Susan Partnow.
whyPurposes: Explore complex topics that resist simple answers or quick decisions
Develop shared understanding across genuine differences of perspective
Create the conditions for deep listening — not just waiting to speak
Surface the values and assumptions that underlie different positions
Make meaning together from confusing or difficult shared experiences
Build the capacity for dialogue rather than debate in a community or organisation
variations: Use a physical talking piece — an object passed around the circle that signals who has the right to speak
Include a formal silence round — 30-60 seconds between each speaker for the group to absorb what was said
Run multiple rounds of increasing depth: first impressions, deeper reactions, then what is essential
Use in conjunction with What, So What, Now What? as a debrief after the Café
In large groups, run parallel Cafés then use Fishbowl to share across groups
examplesOfUse: After a difficult organisational change to help people make sense of what happened and why
In communities where a polarising issue has created division — surfacing shared values across opposing views
In education settings to engage students in deep reflection on ethical or complex topics
In healthcare to process difficult ethical questions that don't have easy answers
As a regular community practice for ongoing sense-making rather than one-time events

name: User Experience Fishbowl
desc: Engage a whole group in a dialogue with a small sample of users, clients, or practitioners who have first-hand experience. The fishbowl format — inner circle speaks, outer circle listens — ensures that user voices are heard without interruption, and that practitioners genuinely absorb what they hear.
when: Use when decision-makers or practitioners have become disconnected from the experience of the people they serve, when user or client perspectives need to inform a design or policy decision, or when a team needs to hear difficult feedback without becoming defensive.

phases.label: Set up fishbowl
phases.instruction: 4-6 users sit in the centre. Everyone else observes from outside. A facilitator manages the inner group.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Welcome our users into the inner circle. Everyone else forms the outer circle. A simple reminder: the inner circle speaks, the outer circle listens — really listens, without preparing rebuttals.

phases.label: Storytelling
phases.instruction: Users tell their stories: what happened when you tried to use our service? The facilitator asks gentle follow-up questions. Observers take notes in silence.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Users: tell us your real experience. What happens when you try to use this service? Walk us through it — the good, the confusing, the frustrating. We are here to learn, not defend.

phases.label: Observer questions
phases.instruction: Observers can enter the inner circle to ask one question each, then return to the outside. No debate, no defence.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Outer circle: now you speak. What did you hear that surprised you? What did you not know? What questions are you sitting with? No explanations — just what you learned.

tips: Prepare inner circle participants beforehand — they should know the format and feel safe to speak honestly.
The outer circle's job is to LISTEN, not to plan their response. Ask them explicitly to take notes on what surprises them, not what they want to say.
Debrief with the outer circle only at first — what did you hear? Then invite inner circle participants back for dialogue.
Trap: practitioners jumping in to explain or defend during the inner circle's time. Enforce the fishbowl boundary firmly.
Trap: inner circle participants softening their experience to protect the feelings of people watching. The host must give explicit permission to be honest.
structuralInvitation: What happens when you try to use our service? Tell us your story — what did you experience?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Include the perspective of users, clients, or patients in design and decision-making
Give voice to people who are rarely heard in formal processes
Build genuine empathy in practitioners and decision-makers through direct listening
Surface the gap between what practitioners believe the experience is and what it actually is
Create accountability for the experience others have of your service or organisation
Generate improvement ideas that are grounded in real experience rather than professional assumptions
variations: Invite multiple groups into the fishbowl sequentially — different types of users, then staff, then managers
Use an empty chair in the inner circle that anyone from the outer circle can join to add a perspective
