Follow with 1-2-4-All: 'What surprised you? What will you do differently?' among the outer circle
Record the inner circle conversation to share with people who couldn't attend
In virtual settings, use a shared document for the outer circle to record reactions without interrupting
examplesOfUse: In service redesign to ensure the new design reflects actual user needs, not assumed ones
In healthcare quality improvement with patients and carers sharing their experience directly with clinical teams
In education with students sharing their learning experience with teachers and curriculum designers
In community development with residents telling planners what they actually need
In policy development with front-line workers telling policymakers what the policy looks like in practice

name: Heard, Seen, Respected
desc: Practice deeply listening to and acknowledging others. By sharing and listening to stories about times when we did not feel heard, seen, or respected, participants develop empathy, build trust, and understand the impact of not being acknowledged — and what it takes to genuinely do so.
when: Use when a team or community needs to build deeper trust and empathy, when people feel unheard or dismissed, or when a group wants to explore issues of inclusion and belonging from lived experience rather than abstract principle.

phases.label: Story sharing
phases.instruction: In pairs: share a time when you did not feel heard, seen, or respected. Speak from personal experience. The listener only listens — no fixing, no sharing their own story.
phases.friendlyInstruction: We are going to share stories from our own experience. The rule is simple: speak in the first person. Share a time when YOU did not feel heard, seen, or respected. No 'we' statements, no general observations — just your story.

phases.label: Reflection
phases.instruction: Listener reflects back what they heard: 'I heard you say...' Check: did you feel heard? Switch roles.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Pairs share their stories. The listener's only job is to listen — no advice, no 'that happened to me too,' no fixing. Just be with the story.

phases.label: Groups of 4
phases.instruction: Two pairs join. What did you notice about listening? What made someone feel heard or not heard?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Back in the full group: what patterns emerged across the stories? Not to solve them — just to name them. What did it feel like to be truly listened to?

phases.label: Whole group
phases.instruction: What conditions create genuine listening? What will you do differently in your conversations going forward?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What does this experience tell us about how we show up for each other day to day? What is one thing you want to do differently?

tips: Establish explicit safety before starting — people are sharing vulnerable experiences.
The instruction is first person only. 'I felt unheard when...' not 'people often feel unheard when...' Enforce this.
Listeners must stay fully present — no note-taking, no phones, no planning their response.
Debrief with the listener, not the storyteller: 'What did you notice in yourself as you listened?'
Trap: stories drifting from personal experience to general observations or complaints. Redirect gently.
Trap: participants choosing surface-level stories to avoid vulnerability. Create space for depth by going first with something real yourself.
structuralInvitation: Share a time when you did not feel heard, seen, or respected. Tell your story from your own experience.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Develop empathy and the ability to truly listen — not just wait to speak
Build trust within teams and communities through shared vulnerability
Understand the lived experience of not being heard, seen, or respected
Create the conditions for people to bring their whole selves to the work
Surface patterns of exclusion that are invisible to those who benefit from the current culture
Practise the skills of acknowledgement that make teams and communities more inclusive
variations: Follow with 1-2-4-All: 'What conditions allow everyone to feel heard, seen, and respected here?'
Use across groups with different roles or backgrounds to reveal different experiences of the same environment
Add a final reflection: 'When did YOU fail to make someone else feel heard, seen, or respected?'
Pair with Wicked Questions to surface the paradoxes in how the group says it values inclusion vs how it acts
Run at team formation or after a significant change to acknowledge the emotional reality of transition
examplesOfUse: In diversity, equity, and inclusion work — moving from concepts to lived experience
At the start of a change initiative to acknowledge the losses and disruptions people are experiencing
In team-building, particularly after conflict or a difficult period
In healthcare, education, or social care settings where feeling unheard is a common complaint
In communities that have experienced trauma or exclusion — creating space to be witnessed

name: Drawing Together
desc: Enable people to access deeper knowledge and insights by drawing on a set of universal symbols — circle, triangle, rectangle, spiral, and person. Drawing bypasses the analytical mind and allows groups to express and share what is difficult to put into words.
when: Use when a group has reached the limits of what verbal language can express, when you want to unlock creative insight, or when you want to make an abstract challenge or vision tangible.

phases.label: Draw your challenge
phases.instruction: Draw your current understanding of the challenge. No writing allowed. Stick figures welcome. Use only basic symbols: circle, triangle, square, star, person, arrow.
phases.friendlyInstruction: You will draw your response to the challenge — but there is a rule: you can only use these five shapes: circle, triangle, square, star, and a simple person. No words allowed. Start now.

phases.label: Gallery and narrate
phases.instruction: Pin drawings up. Each person narrates their drawing in 60 seconds. Others listen without comment.
phases.friendlyInstruction: In your small group, each person shares what they drew and what it means. Look for shared themes or symbols that appeared independently.

phases.label: Patterns
phases.instruction: What patterns appear across the drawings? What do the differences tell us? What assumptions are visible?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What patterns do you see across the drawings? What does the visual language reveal that words might have hidden or complicated?

tips: Give explicit permission to draw badly. The quality of the drawing is irrelevant — the meaning is what matters.
Use the five symbols exactly as specified — the constraint is the point.
Give enough time for people to sit with the prompt before drawing. 1-2 minutes of quiet before anyone picks up a pen.
Debrief with curiosity: 'Tell me about your drawing' not 'What does your drawing mean?'
Trap: participants refusing to engage because they 'can't draw'. Insist: anyone can draw five shapes.
Trap: groups rushing to verbal explanation before really looking at the images. Spend time with the visual before translating it.
structuralInvitation: Draw your response to the challenge using only these basic shapes: a circle, a triangle, a square, a star, and a simple person. No writing allowed.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Reveal insights and perspectives that verbal language cannot easily express
Access tacit and intuitive knowledge that rational analysis often misses
Build shared visual understanding of complex challenges or visions
Create an equal playing field — everyone can draw five shapes, regardless of artistic skill
Make abstract thinking concrete and shareable
Open up creative and non-linear ways of working within groups that have become stuck in analytical patterns
variations: Add colour — each participant uses a different colour to trace their own visual contribution
Create a collective drawing where everyone contributes to the same image simultaneously
Follow with 1-2-4-All: 'What do your drawings have in common? What is different?'
Use sequential drawing: each person starts a drawing, then passes it to the next person to continue
Combine with Improv Prototyping: draw the challenge first, then act it out
examplesOfUse: In strategy work to help a team visualise its future state beyond what bullet points can capture
In conflict resolution to surface each party's understanding of the situation visually
With groups that are over-reliant on analytical thinking and have lost creative connection to their work
In design processes to generate visual prototypes before building physical ones
As a check-in or reflection tool — 'draw how you feel about where we are right now'

name: Design StoryBoards
desc: Co-create a visual, step-by-step design for an interaction, process, or experience. By drawing each step as a storyboard, groups surface the tacit knowledge embedded in experience and make it explicit, shareable, and improvable.
when: Use when designing or improving a service, process, or experience and you want to capture and share the full sequence from the user's perspective. Particularly valuable when there are multiple stakeholders with different mental models of how something works.

phases.label: Set the scene
phases.instruction: Who is the user? What is the context? Define the character and situation together as a group.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about your user — someone specific, not a persona. What does a genuinely great experience look like for them? Start drawing the first moment of their journey.

phases.label: Draw the journey
phases.instruction: In small teams, sketch the user's experience step by step. What do they see, feel, do at each stage? No writing — only drawings.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Continue the storyboard: what happens next? What do they see, feel, and do at each step? Keep drawing — stick figures are fine, this is not about art.

phases.label: Gallery walk
phases.instruction: Post storyboards on the wall. Everyone walks around and leaves sticky note reactions — stars for what's valuable, questions for what's unclear.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Share your storyboard with another pair. What is working? Where does the experience break down? What did the drawing reveal that a written plan would have hidden?

phases.label: Synthesise
phases.instruction: What common elements appear across storyboards? What's the essential experience we're designing for?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What is the single most important moment in your storyboard — the one that makes or breaks the whole experience? What would it take to get that moment right?

tips: Stick figures and simple shapes are sufficient — and better, since they focus attention on the steps rather than the aesthetics.
Work from the user's perspective, not the system's. 'What does the patient experience?' not 'What does the system do?'
Include emotional states in each step — what does the person feel as well as what they do?
Trap: groups jumping to the ideal design before accurately mapping the current state.
Trap: storyboards becoming too abstract — push for the specific moment, not the general category.
Post storyboards visibly so they can be read, challenged, and built on by others.
structuralInvitation: What does a great experience look like for [user]? Draw it step by step — what do they see, feel, and do at each moment?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Develop a richer and more shared understanding of how an interaction or process works in practice
Surface the tacit knowledge that experienced practitioners have but rarely articulate
Make visible the gaps between the intended design and what actually happens
Enable co-design across different roles and perspectives
Create a shared artefact that can be used to brief, train, and improve
Ground abstract design principles in concrete, visible steps
variations: Use split storyboards: current state on the left, ideal state on the right — highlight the gaps
Do rapid prototyping: sketch, share, get feedback, redraw in 15-minute cycles
Add speech bubbles to capture what each person is saying and thinking at each step
Compare storyboards across groups — different groups often reveal completely different mental models
Follow with User Experience Fishbowl: have the 'users' in your storyboard validate whether it reflects reality
examplesOfUse: In service design to map the patient, customer, or citizen journey from their perspective
In training design to plan an effective learning experience step by step
In process improvement to visualise a workflow and identify where it breaks down
In change management to design the transition experience for the people going through it
In product development to prototype user interactions before building anything

name: Celebrity Interview
desc: Discover and build on the wisdom and experience of an expert or leader through a structured, engaging interview conducted in front of the whole group. Unlike a lecture or panel, the Celebrity Interview format keeps the expert's knowledge grounded in real stories and specific experience.
when: Use when an expert, leader, or experienced practitioner has valuable knowledge that the group needs access to, but a standard presentation would make the knowledge abstract and the audience passive. Particularly useful when the expert is a senior leader whose stories are rarely heard in full.

phases.label: Prepare questions
phases.instruction: Audience generates questions using 1-2-4-All. Focus on: what would we most want to know? What would we be afraid to ask?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Introduce your guest to the group. Set the context: why is this person's experience and perspective valuable right now?

phases.label: The interview
phases.instruction: A skilled interviewer draws out the guest's experience with the audience's best questions. Go where the energy goes — don't stick to a script.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Interview your guest with open, curious questions. Think of yourself as a genuinely interested journalist — not a moderator. Follow the interesting threads, not your script.

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: What surprised us? What was most useful? What questions remain?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Open it up: what questions does the wider group have? What do they want to know that the interview did not cover?

tips: The interviewer must prepare thoroughly — the quality of the questions determines the quality of the conversation.
Open with: 'Tell me about a time when...' — stories unlock insight that direct questions about opinion or process don't.
Keep questions short and simple. The expert's answer is the content, not the question.
Trap: the interviewer taking too long to ask each question — keep them brief and move on.
Trap: the expert defaulting to prepared talking points rather than real stories. Follow up: 'Can you give me a specific example?'
Always leave time for the audience to ask their own questions — the most important ones are often not the interviewer's.
structuralInvitation: Tell me about a time when [your work on this topic] went particularly well. What made it possible?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Give the group access to expert knowledge and experience without the passivity of a lecture
Surface the tacit knowledge that experts have but rarely articulate in formal presentations
Create genuine curiosity and engagement — the group learns about a person, not just a topic
Help leaders and experts share their story in a way that builds connection and trust
Democratise expertise — members of the group can ask what they really want to know
Model the value of learning from experience rather than only from formal knowledge
variations: Have participants submit questions in advance — select the most interesting ones and follow up live
Run a 'reverse celebrity interview' where the expert interviews a practitioner about what works on the front line
Use multiple experts in sequence with the same interview format for comparison
Follow with 1-2-4-All: 'What surprised you? What will you do differently based on what you heard?'
In virtual settings, use an audience 'hot seat' where participants can briefly join the interview
examplesOfUse: When a senior leader has stories that the organisation rarely gets to hear in full
To bring external expertise into a team without losing the group's agency to engage and question
In learning programmes to connect participants with practitioners from the field
To surface the experience and wisdom of long-serving or retiring staff members before they leave
In community settings to honour and learn from elders or experienced members

name: Social Network Webbing
desc: Map informal connections to identify who is in the network, what connects them, and where the missing links are. Making the informal network visible reveals who has influence, where information flows (and where it doesn't), and how to strengthen the network to support change.
when: Use when launching or accelerating a change initiative, when information isn't reaching the people who need it, or when you need to understand who the real connectors and influencers are — as opposed to who holds formal authority.

phases.label: Map the network
phases.instruction: Place name cards on a large surface. Draw connections between people who regularly share information or collaborate. Use different colours for strong/weak ties.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about how your work actually gets done — not the org chart, but the real flow. Who do you actually go to for information, advice, or to get things moving? Write their names.

phases.label: Analyse
phases.instruction: Where are the bridges (connectors between clusters)? Where are the silos? Who is over-connected and at risk of burnout?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Map your connections on the shared diagram. Draw lines to the people you named. Thick lines for frequent contact, thin for occasional.

phases.label: Plan
phases.instruction: What connections need to be strengthened? What new bridges need to be built? What structures are creating silos?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Look at the map together. Where are the hubs — the people everything flows through? Where are the gaps — places where connection is missing but needed? What surprises you?

tips: Ask specifically: 'Who do you go to for help with your work?' rather than 'who do you work with?' — this reveals real influence.
Draw lines of different weights or colours to represent frequency and type of connection.
Protect confidentiality — individual maps should not be shared without consent.
Trap: the map becoming the org chart because people name their formal colleagues rather than their real connections.
Trap: treating the map as a final product rather than the start of a conversation about what it reveals.
Use the map to ask: 'Where are we well-connected? Where are we isolated? Who is at risk of overload?'
structuralInvitation: Who do you regularly turn to for information, advice, or collaboration to get your work done? Let's map the real connections.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Identify and develop the informal network that actually gets things done
Surface connectors, bridges, and isolated nodes that are invisible in the org chart
Reveal the flow of information and resources across formal boundaries
Identify the critical relationships that need strengthening for a change to succeed
Build on existing connections rather than creating parallel structures
Make visible the informal leadership that formal leaders often can't see
variations: Use different types of connections for different questions: trust networks, information networks, advice networks
Do before-and-after mappings to assess whether an intervention has strengthened connections
Use network analysis software for large groups (>50) to visualise patterns systematically
Combine with Appreciative Interviews to discover the stories behind the strongest relationships
Follow with Shift & Share: people with the strongest networks share what makes them effective
examplesOfUse: In change management to identify the informal leaders who will make or break implementation
In community development to understand and strengthen existing social capital
In knowledge management to map where expertise actually lives, not where the org chart says it should
In healthcare to understand how clinical teams actually coordinate across ward boundaries
At the start of a large cross-functional project to identify who needs to be connected

name: What I Need From You (WINFY)
desc: Surface and address the needs that people in different roles or groups have of each other. Using only four permitted responses — Yes, No, I'll try, or Whatever — WINFY cuts through vague complaints and unspoken expectations to create clear, actionable commitments.
when: Use when groups or departments depend on each other but have accumulated unspoken resentments, when cross-boundary coordination is breaking down, or when people have needs that they've been reluctant to name explicitly.

phases.label: Brief and set context
phases.instruction: Reiterate the shared goal or challenge being addressed — context must be the same for all. Explain the four permitted responses: YES, NO, I WILL TRY, and WHATEVER (meaning: your request was too vague to answer specifically). No other responses are allowed. No elaboration or discussion when responding. Position the functional clusters around the room.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Before we start, let's be clear about the shared goal we're all working toward. Then the rules: when you respond to a request, you can only say YES, NO, I WILL TRY, or WHATEVER — which means "your request was too vague for me to give you a real answer." No explanations. No negotiation. No "but what I meant was..." Clear requests get clear answers.

phases.label: Craft top needs
phases.instruction: Functional clusters use 1-2-4-All to identify their top needs from each of the other groups in the room. Needs are expressed as requests in the form: "What I need from you is ____." Each cluster reduces their list to their two most important needs, writes them down in exact form, and selects a spokesperson.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All in your group: what do you truly need from each of the other groups here in order to succeed? Complete the sentence: "What I need from you is ___." Be specific — vague requests get "WHATEVER" as an answer. Reduce to your two most important needs per group, write them down precisely, and choose your spokesperson.

phases.label: Express needs — no responses yet
phases.instruction: Spokespeople come to the centre (inner circle). Each spokesperson reads their needs to the relevant groups. Receiving groups ONLY LISTEN — no responses yet, no reactions. This is strictly enforced.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Spokespeople: come to the centre. Each of you will read your needs aloud to the groups you're addressing. Everyone else: your only job right now is to listen. No reactions, no nodding, no "mmm". Strictly listen. You'll get to respond shortly.
phases.groups: Spokespeople in inner circle; others listen

phases.label: Consult and formulate responses
phases.instruction: Spokespeople return to their groups. Each group confers and decides on their response to each request directed at them: YES, NO, I WILL TRY, or WHATEVER. No other options. Groups select their response and prepare to deliver it without elaboration.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Spokespeople: go back to your groups. Discuss the requests directed at you and decide: YES, NO, I WILL TRY, or WHATEVER. Choose one — you cannot negotiate or qualify it when you deliver it. No "it depends" or "we would if..." Just the answer.

phases.label: Deliver responses
phases.instruction: Spokespeople reconvene in the inner circle. Each spokesperson delivers their responses to each request, one at a time, without elaboration. No discussion allowed at this stage.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Spokespeople: back to the centre. Deliver your answers one by one — just the answer, nothing more. No explaining. No softening. The directness is the point.
phases.groups: Spokespeople in inner circle; others listen

phases.label: Debrief
phases.instruction: Debrief with What, So What, Now What? What surprised us? What requests got "WHATEVER" — what does that tell us about how clearly we express our needs? What commitments emerged? What was not asked that should have been?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What surprised you? Which responses were unexpected? Any "WHATEVER" answers — what does that tell you about how clearly you're asking? What commitments did you hear that you want to hold each other to? And: what did you need but didn't ask for?

tips: The four responses are sacred. No elaboration, no 'yes but', no 'it depends'. The discipline is the point.
Groups must have time to consult before responding — don't ask for immediate on-the-spot responses.
Requests must be specific enough to merit a yes or no. 'Be more supportive' gets 'Whatever'. 'Share the project status every Friday' gets a real answer.
Trap: groups using 'I'll try' to avoid a real yes or no. Challenge: 'What would need to be true for this to be a yes?'
Trap: the listening round becoming a discussion. Enforce: requests are heard in silence, responses come later.
Follow with clear documentation of commitments — verbal agreements without a record rarely hold.
structuralInvitation: What I need from you to be successful in achieving [shared goal] is... (complete the sentence with a clear, specific request)
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Kathie Dannemiller and professor Dan Pesut.
whyPurposes: Give voice to the needs that people have of each other but rarely express clearly
Replace vague complaints and resentments with concrete, actionable requests
Create the conditions for honest response — yes, no, or an honest 'I'll try'
Surface the dependencies between roles and groups that everyone knows about but no one discusses
Build clarity about what people can and cannot commit to
Strengthen working relationships through honest, direct communication
variations: Use 'HUH?' instead of 'Whatever' to signal a request that was too vague — it's more lovingly provocative
Run a follow-up session 30 days later: how have the commitments held?
Combine with What, So What, Now What? as a debrief after the responses are given
Use anonymised written requests first, then reveal who made each request in the group response round
In virtual settings, post needs in a shared document, then groups respond in breakout rooms
examplesOfUse: Between departments or teams that have interdependencies and unresolved frustrations
Between managers and their direct reports to surface what both need from each other
In cross-functional projects when role clarity and coordination have broken down
Between clinical and administrative teams in healthcare where handoffs consistently fail
At the start of a new partnership or collaboration to set explicit expectations from the outset

name: Open Space Technology
desc: Invite a group of any size to organise itself and create their own agenda around a theme of strategic importance. Open Space self-organises around passion and responsibility — participants call sessions on topics they genuinely care about and take personal responsibility for.
when: Use when the right agenda cannot be predicted in advance, when the group has more wisdom than any single planner, when you need genuine commitment rather than compliance, or when a group needs to tackle a complex challenge that spans multiple perspectives and roles.

phases.label: Opening circle
phases.instruction: Gather the whole group in a circle. The convener introduces the theme and the four principles: whoever comes is the right people, whenever it starts is the right time, whatever happens is the only thing that could, when it's over it's over.
phases.friendlyInstruction: If you have a question or topic you feel genuinely passionate about and personally responsible for exploring — come up and post it. You are voting with your feet when you choose sessions.

phases.label: Marketplace
phases.instruction: Anyone who wants to convene a session announces it and posts it on the wall. Sessions self-organise into available spaces and times.
phases.friendlyInstruction: You have your marketplace of sessions. The Law of Two Feet applies: if you are not learning or contributing, move. No guilt — movement is encouraged.

phases.label: Sessions
phases.instruction: Concurrent self-organised sessions run in parallel. Participants vote with their feet — move if you're not learning or contributing. Conveners take notes.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Work is happening in parallel sessions right now. Follow your energy. Take notes on what emerges — the harvest comes at the end.

phases.label: Evening news
phases.instruction: Brief summaries of what happened in each session. What insights are emerging?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Each session host shares the key insights, questions, and commitments from their group. What will we actually do with what was discovered?

phases.label: Closing circle
phases.instruction: What are we taking away? What actions will we commit to? What remains open?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Looking at everything that emerged today: what surprised you? What feels most important? What are you now committed to?

tips: The opening question or invitation must be compelling — it determines the energy and quality of the self-organised sessions.
Trust the Law of Two Feet completely. Bumblebees and butterflies are valuable — resist the urge to manage who goes where.
The harvest is as important as the sessions. Build in structured time to share and commit to what emerged.
Trap: facilitators trying to manage or guide which sessions happen. Step back entirely.
Trap: leadership deciding in advance which topics are 'acceptable'. Open Space only works with genuine openness.
Plan for surprises — they are a feature, not a bug. The most important conversations are usually the ones nobody planned.
structuralInvitation: What questions or issues do you most care about, for which you have genuine passion and real responsibility to act?
attribution: Developed by Harrison Owen. Included in the Liberating Structures repertoire by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Enable self-organisation around what participants are genuinely passionate and responsible for
Tap collective intelligence across an entire organisation, community, or system
Generate real commitment — people work on what they chose, not what was assigned
Surface the real conversations that need to happen rather than the ones that were planned
Build cross-boundary connections around shared interests and challenges
Create the conditions for emergence — outcomes that no one could have planned in advance
variations: Use the Law of Two Feet explicitly — if you're not learning or contributing, it's your responsibility to move
Combine with 1-2-4-All at the end of each session to harvest insights before they disperse
Post a digital marketplace board for virtual Open Space sessions
Run a 'mini Open Space' of 60-90 minutes by limiting sessions to 15-20 minutes each
Follow with Purpose-to-Practice to move from discovered energy to structural commitment
examplesOfUse: At whole-organisation summits to set priorities and generate commitment without top-down agenda-setting
In communities to enable citizens to set the agenda for complex local challenges
In professional conferences to replace passive presentations with active peer-to-peer learning
At the start of a major change initiative to surface what people are most concerned about and most committed to
In networks and coalitions where no single body has authority to set the agenda for everyone

name: Generative Relationships STAR
desc: Reveal relationship patterns that are inhibiting or enabling progress. Using four dimensions — Separateness, Tuning, Acting Together, and Reason to Return — groups assess and improve the generative quality of their relationships with partners, stakeholders, or each other.
when: Use when a partnership or collaborative relationship is underperforming, when groups feel that 'the relationship isn't working' but can't pinpoint why, or when you want to intentionally design more generative connections.

phases.label: Assess individually
phases.instruction: Each person rates the relationship on four dimensions: Separate (maintaining identity), Tuning (paying attention), Acting (taking initiative), Reciprocating (giving back). Use 1-5 scale.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Look at the four STAR dimensions together: Separateness, Tuning, Acting Together, and Reason to Return. Without judging, where would you honestly place your relationship on each one right now?

phases.label: Share and compare
phases.instruction: Share your ratings and reasoning. Where do you agree? Where do you see things differently? Explore the differences with curiosity.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Share your ratings and the reasoning behind them. Where do you agree? Where do you see it differently? The gaps are where the most interesting conversation lives.

phases.label: Plan
phases.instruction: What small shifts in each dimension would make this relationship more generative? What will you each do differently?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Given where you are on the STAR dimensions, what would move this relationship in a more generative direction? What is one specific thing each party could do differently?

tips: The four dimensions — Separateness, Tuning, Acting Together, Reason to Return — need to be explained with concrete examples before rating.
Individual ratings are more honest than group ratings — collect individually, then share and discuss.
Focus on what can be improved rather than attributing blame for the current state.
Trap: groups conflating 'Reason to Return' with just enjoying each other's company — it means genuine mutual benefit.
Trap: treating the assessment as a verdict rather than a starting point. The point is to improve, not to judge.
The most generative dimension to improve first is usually 'Tuning' — learning to notice and respond to each other's signals.
structuralInvitation: How generative is our relationship? Where do we stand on each of the four STAR dimensions: Separateness, Tuning, Acting Together, Reason to Return?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the work of Glenda Eoyang.
whyPurposes: Diagnose and improve the generative quality of relationships and partnerships
Surface the specific dimensions of relationship that are enabling or inhibiting progress
Build shared language for talking about relationship quality beyond 'it's not working'
Identify concrete actions to improve relationships rather than just describing problems
Help groups see that relationship quality is designable, not just emergent
Enable honest conversation about what each party needs to contribute to a more generative relationship
variations: Use with multiple relationships simultaneously — map where you are on STAR with different partners
Do individual STAR assessments first, then compare across the group — the gaps reveal blind spots
Follow with Min Specs: what are the minimum specifications for a generative relationship in this context?
Use before and after a relationship intervention to measure change
Combine with Social Network Webbing to see which relationships in the network are most generative
examplesOfUse: In multi-agency partnerships to diagnose why coordination is not producing results
In joint ventures or alliances to develop relationship quality as a deliberate strategy
In communities of practice to assess whether the community is becoming more or less generative over time
In team development to surface what each member needs from the team to contribute fully
In complex systems change work where the quality of relationships between organisations matters as much as the strategies

name: Agreement-Certainty Matrix
desc: Move from analysis to action based on the nature of the challenge. By plotting challenges according to how much agreement exists and how certain you are about cause and effect, groups discover which problems require simple management, which require complexity thinking, and which require something entirely different.
when: Use when a group is applying the same approach to all its challenges regardless of whether that approach fits, when experts disagree about a problem and can't agree why, or when a complex challenge is being managed as if it were simple.

phases.label: Map your challenges
phases.instruction: List your current challenges and place each in one of four zones based on agreement and certainty levels among stakeholders.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Look at the challenge you are facing. On the matrix: how much genuine agreement is there among the people involved? And how certain are you about what causes what? Place it honestly.

phases.label: Discuss placements
phases.instruction: Share your mappings. Where do you disagree about where a challenge belongs? The disagreements are often the most important conversations.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Compare where different people placed the same challenge. Where do you disagree about the level of agreement or certainty? That disagreement is itself important information.

phases.label: Match approaches
phases.instruction: For each zone, what's the right approach? Simple = best practice. Complicated = good practice. Complex = emergent practice. Chaotic = novel practice.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Given where your challenges sit on the matrix, what kind of approach actually fits? Simple problems need different methods than complex or chaotic ones — what does your placement suggest?

tips: Define the two axes clearly with examples before asking groups to place challenges — the concepts of agreement and certainty need concrete illustration.
Individual placements first, then group comparison — the disagreements are where the learning is.
Trap: groups treating the matrix as a permanent verdict. Problems migrate — a well-managed complex challenge can become simple.
Trap: using the Complicated quadrant as a dumping ground for things the group doesn't know how to approach.
Connect explicitly to action: 'This challenge is in the Complex zone — which LS or approach fits?'
structuralInvitation: Where does each of your current challenges fall on the matrix? How much agreement is there among stakeholders, and how much certainty do you have about cause and effect?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Cynefin framework (Dave Snowden).
whyPurposes: Help groups match their approach to the real nature of each challenge
Stop applying simple solutions to complex problems — and complex frameworks to simple ones
Surface hidden disagreement about what kind of problem the group is actually facing
Build shared language for complexity and uncertainty across different levels and functions
Enable better decision-making by clarifying what is actually known and agreed
Connect to action: each quadrant suggests a different leadership and management response
variations: Plot multiple challenges simultaneously and compare — groups are often surprised by the spread
Use as an ongoing diagnostic rather than a one-time exercise — challenges migrate across the matrix over time
Combine with Critical Uncertainties: use the matrix to identify which uncertainties matter most
Follow with different LS depending on where challenges land — simple (best practice), complex (DAD, TRIZ), chaotic (Improv Prototyping)
In large groups, have subgroups independently place challenges and then compare — the disagreements are as informative as the placements
examplesOfUse: In strategy development to distinguish which aspects of the strategy are simple execution and which require emergent approaches
In leadership development to build complexity literacy — the capacity to diagnose and respond to different types of problems
In quality improvement to understand which defects are caused by process failure (simple) vs systemic complexity
In policy development where different stakeholders have fundamentally different views of the problem
In consulting work to help clients see why their technically correct solution is not working in a complex context

name: Simple Ethnography
desc: Observe and record the actual behaviours and interactions of people in the field to discover what is really happening, as opposed to what is assumed to be happening. Going to see for yourself — with genuine curiosity and minimal assumptions — is the foundation of human-centred improvement.
when: Use when decisions are being made based on assumptions about what users, clients, or colleagues experience rather than direct observation. Particularly valuable when there is a significant gap between how a service or process is designed and how it actually works.

phases.label: Prepare
phases.instruction: What will you observe? What questions will guide your observation? Prepare an observation guide. Remember: you are there to learn, not to fix.
phases.friendlyInstruction: We are going out to observe, not to evaluate. Your job is to watch and listen as a genuine learner — not as an expert, not as an auditor. Note what you actually see and hear, not what you expect.

phases.label: Observe
phases.instruction: Visit the workplace or service point. Observe and take notes. Watch what people actually do, not what they say they do. Take photos if permitted.
phases.friendlyInstruction: In the field: observe without intervening. Write down specific moments, quotes, and details — not your interpretations of them. What is actually happening?

phases.label: Debrief with host
phases.instruction: Thank your hosts. Share one observation that surprised you. Ask what they wish decision-makers knew about their work.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Back together: share your raw observations first, before any interpretation. What did you see? What did you hear? What did you not expect?

phases.label: Sense-making
phases.instruction: With your partner: what did you observe? So what does it mean? Now what should change?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Now interpret: what do the observations mean? What do they suggest about how things actually work, as opposed to how we assumed they worked?

tips: Observe with genuine curiosity, not the aim of confirming what you already think. Leave your hypotheses behind.
Take notes on specific, observable moments — not interpretations. 'The nurse looked at the screen, not the patient' not 'the nurse was distracted'.
Debrief separately: raw observations first, then interpretations — keep them clearly distinguished.
Trap: observers narrating their mental models rather than what they actually see. Challenge each statement: 'Did you see that, or did you infer it?'
Trap: people being observed changing their behaviour because they know they're being watched. Spend enough time that this fades.
Be transparent with the people you are observing — explain what you're doing and why. Trust enables honesty.
structuralInvitation: What is really happening when people do this work? Let's go observe for ourselves — as learners, not as inspectors.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by ethnographic research traditions in social science.
whyPurposes: Develop empathy and understanding for the people you serve through direct, unmediated observation
Surface the gap between how you think your system works and how it actually works
Discover local solutions and positive deviance that desk-based analysis would miss
Build the capacity to see your work from the perspective of the people most affected
Ground decisions and improvements in real data from the field rather than assumptions
Connect practitioners to the human impact of their work
variations: Use guided observation with a simple template: what I expected to see / what I actually saw / what surprised me
Have observers from different roles observe the same situation to compare what they notice
Combine with Appreciative Interviews after observation: 'Tell me about a time this worked well'
Follow with DAD to discover what the people you observed are already doing that works
Use before and after an improvement to measure whether the change made a real difference to the experience
examplesOfUse: In healthcare improvement — go to the ward and watch what actually happens, not what the protocol says
In service design — follow a customer through their entire journey rather than just surveying them at the end
In education — observe a classroom before redesigning curriculum
In community development — spend time in the community before proposing interventions
In organisational development — shadow a front-line worker for a day before designing training for them

name: Integrated~Autonomy
desc: Move from either/or to both/and when facing the tension between local autonomy and central integration. By exploring what would make both more possible simultaneously, groups discover structural innovations that neither pure autonomy nor pure control can produce.
when: Use when an organisation is swinging between centralisation and decentralisation, when local teams feel over-controlled while the centre feels under-informed, or when the tension between standardisation and local adaptation is blocking progress.

phases.label: Generate both sides
phases.instruction: In two parallel groups: one generates all the reasons for more autonomy, the other for more integration. Be bold — don't self-censor.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about what you need that is more locally controlled AND more centrally coordinated — simultaneously. Not a compromise, but genuinely both. Write down the tensions you are holding.

phases.label: Share and listen
phases.instruction: Each group presents. The other group listens for what's genuinely valuable in the opposing view. No debate yet.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Using 1-2-4-All, explore: what would enable more local autonomy without losing the benefits of integration? And more integration without losing local ownership?

phases.label: Both/And
phases.instruction: Together: what strategies would give us more of both? What conditions enable both autonomy and integration at the same time?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What specific structures, agreements, or practices could make both more possible at the same time? What would you need to stop doing to make room?

phases.label: Commit
phases.instruction: What specific changes will you make? Which practices enable both dimensions simultaneously?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Name your commitments: what will you actually do to move toward integration AND autonomy? Who needs to be involved that is not in this room?

tips: The tension must be real and felt by the group — don't use this on theoretical or low-stakes questions.
Both sides of the tension (autonomy AND integration) must be genuinely valued before the exercise can work.
Trap: groups collapsing back to either/or thinking — 'let's just have more autonomy'. Hold the AND firmly.
Trap: abstract solutions that sound good but specify nothing. Push for concrete structural examples.
The 'Integrated~Autonomy' name is deliberately paradoxical — the tilde (~) signals 'both at the same time, not just a balance'.
structuralInvitation: What do we need that provides both more local autonomy AND more central integration simultaneously? What would make both more possible at the same time?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Explore and navigate the tension between local autonomy and central integration creatively
Move beyond false either/or choices about how to organise
Discover structural and relational innovations that enable both independence and coordination
Surface the hidden costs of both extremes — too much control and too little
Build shared understanding of what each level needs from the others
Enable distributed leadership without losing the benefits of coordination
variations: Use 1-2-4-All within each step for groups larger than 8
Map the current state on a spectrum from pure autonomy to pure integration before exploring what both would look like
Follow with Min Specs: what are the minimum non-negotiables that enable both autonomy and integration?
Combine with WINFY: what does the centre need from the local? What does local need from the centre?
Use across levels — have local teams and central teams do the exercise separately, then share
examplesOfUse: In federated organisations (NHS, multi-site businesses, school networks) navigating the centre/local tension
In professional services firms where standardisation and professional judgment are in constant tension
In franchises balancing brand consistency with local market adaptation
In community organisations where member autonomy and collective action need to coexist
In global companies navigating the global/local tension in ways that neither pure standardisation nor pure localisation can resolve

name: Critical Uncertainties
desc: Prepare for the future by exploring the most critical and unknowable uncertainties your work faces. By constructing four scenarios from two critical uncertainties and developing robust strategies for each, groups become genuinely prepared for multiple possible futures rather than betting on one.
when: Use when the future is genuinely uncertain and planning based on a single forecast is risky, when a group needs to test whether its current strategy is fragile or robust, or when key assumptions about the future need to be examined openly.

phases.label: Identify uncertainties
phases.instruction: Generate all the important uncertainties facing your initiative. Reduce to the 2 most critical — those with highest impact and highest uncertainty.
phases.friendlyInstruction: List all the factors that could significantly affect the success of your work. Now identify the two that are both most impactful AND most uncertain. These are your critical uncertainties.

phases.label: Create scenario matrix
phases.instruction: Place your two critical uncertainties on a 2x2 matrix. Name each of the four scenarios that results.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Plot your two critical uncertainties on a 2×2 matrix. This creates four possible future scenarios. Give each scenario a name that captures its essential character.

phases.label: Explore scenarios
phases.instruction: In small groups, deeply explore each scenario. What would the world look like? What would be true? What would you need to do?
phases.friendlyInstruction: For each scenario: what would it mean for your work? What would you need to do differently? What early signals would tell you this scenario was emerging?

phases.label: Robust strategies
phases.instruction: Which strategies work across multiple scenarios? What are the early indicators that tell you which scenario is unfolding?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Looking across all four scenarios: what strategies would serve you well in any of them? What would you do right now that makes sense regardless of which future unfolds?

tips: The two selected uncertainties must be genuinely important AND genuinely uncertain — not things you can reasonably predict.
The four scenario names should be vivid and memorable — they will be used throughout the planning process.
Focus equal time on all four scenarios — groups tend to spend most time on their preferred or feared future.
Trap: selecting uncertainties that are really different versions of the same uncertainty.
Trap: treating the scenario work as forecasting rather than preparation. The goal is strategy that works across scenarios, not identifying the 'right' scenario.
The most valuable output is often the list of early signals that would tell you which scenario is emerging.
structuralInvitation: What are the most critical unknowns — the factors with the highest impact and highest uncertainty — that could most affect the success of our work?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by scenario planning methods.
whyPurposes: Help a group develop strategies that are robust across multiple possible futures
Surface and challenge hidden assumptions about what the future will look like
Move from prediction to preparedness — building capacity to adapt rather than predict
Reveal which current strategies are fragile (only work if one future arrives) and which are robust
Create shared language for uncertainty without requiring everyone to agree on a forecast
Build the capacity to hold multiple futures simultaneously rather than defaulting to the most comfortable one
variations: Use with senior leadership teams to stress-test a strategy rather than generate a new one
Combine with Agreement-Certainty Matrix to identify which uncertainties are truly in the chaotic zone
Run in parallel groups, each developing scenarios from different pairs of uncertainties — compare across groups
Follow with Purpose-to-Practice to develop organisational design that works across scenarios
Use with external partners and stakeholders who have different views of which uncertainties are most critical
examplesOfUse: In strategic planning to test whether the strategy holds across different possible futures
In public policy to design interventions that are effective regardless of which way key uncertainties resolve
In healthcare planning where demographic, technological, and political uncertainties are all high
In environmental work where climate scenarios interact with social and economic uncertainties
In start-ups and new ventures to plan for the different ways the market might evolve

name: Ecocycle Planning
desc: Identify where your portfolio of activities and initiatives are in their natural lifecycle and plan accordingly. By mapping current work across four phases — Birth, Maturity, Creative Destruction, and Renewal — groups discover what to start, what to stop, and what to transform.
when: Use in annual planning, strategic reviews, or any point where a group needs to look at its full portfolio of activities honestly and decide what to invest in, what to wind down, and what new initiatives to launch.

phases.label: Map activities
phases.instruction: List all current activities, projects, and initiatives on sticky notes. Then place each on the Ecocycle map: Birth, Maturity, Creative Destruction, or Renewal.
phases.friendlyInstruction: List all your current activities and initiatives. Now honestly: where is each one in its lifecycle? Growing? Mature and efficient? Declining? Stuck? Place each one on the Ecocycle map.

phases.label: Identify traps
phases.instruction: Where are you trapped in a Poverty Trap (nothing new starting) or Rigidity Trap (can't let go of mature activities)? Mark these.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Look at your map: where is the Creative Destruction needed — what needs to end or radically transform so something new can begin? What are you keeping alive out of habit, not value?

phases.label: Whole group review
phases.instruction: Share your maps. What patterns do you see across the organisation? Where are most resources going?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Where are the opportunities for renewal? What new initiatives or approaches are waiting in the wings? What would need to stop for them to have space?

phases.label: Plan moves
phases.instruction: What will you start? What will you stop? What will you accelerate? Make specific decisions.
phases.friendlyInstruction: What specific decisions follow from this map? What will you stop, start, or fundamentally change? Name them.

phases.label: Commitments
phases.instruction: Each team commits to specific moves: at least one stop and one start.
phases.friendlyInstruction: How will you revisit this map? The Ecocycle is a living picture, not a one-time exercise — what is your commitment to using it?

tips: The Creative Destruction trap (the 'poverty trap' and 'rigidity trap' on the Ecocycle) is where most organisations get stuck — make sure to name these explicitly.
Individual placements first reveal the real diversity of views about where things are in their lifecycle.
The hardest and most valuable conversation is about what is in the 'Conservation/Maintenance' phase that should be moving into Creative Destruction.
Trap: the map becoming an intellectual exercise rather than leading to real decisions about what to stop, continue, and start.
Trap: senior leaders using authority to move things on the map rather than engaging with the evidence.
The map is a starting point for conversation, not a final verdict — expect and welcome disagreement.
structuralInvitation: Where are each of your current activities and initiatives in their natural lifecycle? Place each one on the Ecocycle map.
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by the Ecocycle model (C.S. Holling) and Panarchy theory.
whyPurposes: Help groups see their portfolio of activities as a living ecosystem with natural lifecycles
Surface activities that have passed their peak and are now consuming resources without proportionate value
Identify the 'creative destruction' that must happen before renewal is possible
Enable deliberate conversation about stopping things — not just about what to start
Plan with the full spectrum of lifecycle phases in mind, not just launch and maintain
Build the capacity to let go as well as to grow
variations: Use individual placements followed by group comparison and discussion of disagreements
Add resource flow lines — show where time, money, and attention are flowing to reveal invisible priorities
Focus on a specific domain (services, projects, partnerships) rather than the full portfolio
Run annually as a living review rather than a one-time exercise
Combine with Min Specs: 'What are the minimum conditions required for this activity to justify its place on the map?'
examplesOfUse: In annual planning to make visible which initiatives are ready to scale, which are past their prime, and which are ready to be born
In portfolio management to balance investment across different lifecycle phases
In organisational design when the structure has accumulated legacy elements that no longer serve the purpose
In community organisations to review what programmes and activities still serve the community's real needs
In health systems to identify which services need to be transformed and which decommissioned to free resources for new needs

name: Panarchy
desc: Understand how events happening at different levels of a system influence each other. By mapping what is happening at the individual, group, organisational, and wider ecosystem level, groups discover the leverage points where action at one level can catalyse positive change across levels.
when: Use when a challenge seems intractable because it spans multiple levels simultaneously, when local actions seem to have no impact on the larger system, or when system-level trends are overwhelming local action.

phases.label: Map the levels
phases.instruction: Identify the different scales of your system (individual, team, department, organisation, ecosystem). What is happening at each level?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Think about the different scales of your system: what is happening at the individual level? The team level? The organisation level? The wider ecosystem? Describe what you see at each level.

phases.label: Identify connections
phases.instruction: How do actions at one level affect other levels? Where are the 'revolt' connections (small disrupting large) and 'remember' connections (large constraining small)?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Where do the scales interact? How do actions at one level ripple through the others — up and down? Where is the leverage? Where are the traps?

phases.label: Plan across scales
phases.instruction: What actions at which level would have the most leverage? How can you act locally while thinking at multiple scales?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Given what you see, what actions at which level would have the most positive impact across the whole system? What would you stop doing at one level to create room at another?

tips: Start by grounding each level in specific, concrete examples rather than abstract descriptions.
The 'revolt' (small-scale disruption changing large-scale patterns) and 'remember' (large-scale patterns constraining small-scale innovation) connections are often the most revealing.
Trap: staying descriptive rather than identifying action. Always move from 'this is what's happening at each level' to 'where can we act to create positive cross-scale effects?'
Trap: groups only focusing on the level they work at rather than genuinely exploring all levels.
This is one of the most cognitively demanding LS — allow time and give permission to sit with complexity.
structuralInvitation: What is happening at different scales of your system — individual, team, organisation, wider ecosystem? How do these levels interact and influence each other?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless. Inspired by Panarchy theory (Holling, Gunderson, and Allen).
whyPurposes: Connect micro-level actions to macro-level patterns and system dynamics
Reveal the nested and interdependent cycles that shape how systems change over time
Identify the leverage points where action at one scale can catalyse change at others
Build a more sophisticated understanding of why some interventions work and others don't
Enable groups to act locally while understanding the wider system they are part of
Prepare for the cross-scale surprises — the 'revolt' and 'remember' connections — that often derail well-planned initiatives
variations: Draw the Ecocycle at each level to show where each level is in its development
Use different colours to show flow of influence — green for positive, red for constraining
Map historical changes to understand how the system has evolved, not just how it is now
Combine with Critical Uncertainties: which cross-scale dynamics are most uncertain and most impactful?
Follow with Integrated~Autonomy to develop strategies that work across levels
examplesOfUse: In complex social issues where individual behaviour, community norms, and national policy all interact
In healthcare reform where clinical practice, organisational culture, and system policy are all relevant
In education reform where classroom practice, school culture, and national curriculum interact
In community development where local action, regional infrastructure, and global economic trends all affect outcomes
In environmental work where individual choices, corporate behaviour, and policy frameworks must all shift

name: Purpose-to-Practice
desc: Define the five elements that are essential for a resilient and enduring initiative: Purpose, Principles, Participants, Structure, and Practices. Moving through each element in sequence — and revisiting them as the design evolves — ensures that what gets built is grounded in shared purpose rather than just activity.
when: Use when founding a new organisation, initiative, or community of practice; when redesigning an existing one; or when a group has lost its connection to why it exists and needs to return to first principles.

phases.label: Purpose
phases.instruction: What is our deepest aspiration? What are we here to do? Use 1-2-4-All to craft a purpose statement everyone believes in.
phases.friendlyInstruction: What is our deepest aspiration — our purpose? Not a mission statement. The real reason this work matters. Take your time with this one.

phases.label: Principles
phases.instruction: What values and principles will guide all our decisions? Use Min Specs thinking — only include what's truly essential.
phases.friendlyInstruction: Given that purpose, what principles will guide how we work together? Not rules — the values that should show up in our day-to-day decisions.

phases.label: Participants
phases.instruction: Who must be included? Who are all the stakeholders? What are their roles? Who is missing from this conversation?
phases.friendlyInstruction: Who needs to be part of this? Who has a stake in the purpose and brings something essential? Think beyond the obvious people.

phases.label: Structure
phases.instruction: How will we organise ourselves? What decisions can be made by whom? How will we communicate?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What specific structures will enable the purpose? Meetings, roles, decision-making processes — what needs to exist and how should it work?

phases.label: Practices
phases.instruction: What specific activities and routines will bring our purpose to life? What will we do differently starting tomorrow?
phases.friendlyInstruction: What practices will we commit to? The regular activities that keep us connected to the purpose and accountable to each other?

tips: Work through the five elements in order — each depends on the previous. Jumping ahead produces structures that aren't grounded.
The Purpose element deserves the most time — 'to make a difference' is not a purpose. Push for specificity and genuine shared meaning.
Principles should be actionable — 'we value respect' is not a principle. 'We speak directly with each other before speaking about each other' is.
Trap: the group designing for what they think they should be rather than what they genuinely commit to.
Trap: rushing through the elements to get to structure and practices — these are the easiest to design and the most dangerous to design first.
Revisit regularly — organisations that only do Purpose-to-Practice once tend to drift back to old patterns within a year.
structuralInvitation: What is our deepest aspiration — our purpose? Why does this work matter, and what must we do to bring it to life?
attribution: Developed by Henri Lipmanowicz and Keith McCandless.
whyPurposes: Define the elements that are necessary and sufficient for a robust and lasting initiative
Build genuine shared ownership by involving everyone in co-creating the design
Ensure that structure and practices are derived from purpose and principles rather than inherited by default
Prevent the common failure mode of organisations that are busy but disconnected from purpose
Create alignment without uniformity — a shared foundation that supports diverse action
Make the invisible architecture of how a group works together visible and subject to intentional design
variations: Revisit the five elements at regular intervals — purpose and principles tend to drift without deliberate maintenance
Run each element in a separate session for complex or large-scale initiatives
Use 1-2-4-All within each element to ensure broad participation in the design
Combine with Min Specs to identify the absolute non-negotiables within each element
Follow with Critical Uncertainties: which aspects of the design depend on uncertainties that need scenario planning?
examplesOfUse: When founding a new social enterprise, network, or community organisation
At the start of a major change initiative to build genuine commitment rather than just compliance
When a successful initiative has grown and needs more explicit organisational design
When a team has lost its sense of purpose and needs to reconnect with why it exists
In governance design for collaborative or multi-stakeholder bodies
